Here are some random ideas on breaking up the code a bit. They might be a bridge too far in refactoring.

1) The long list of command strings that are just at the beginning of the file could be turned into small class objects, in a dedicated file.

For instance:

```
class PlayGet(RXVRequest):
   zone_cmd = False
   method = "GET"

   def __init__(self, source):
       self.source = source

   def __str__(self, zone=None):
       return "<{src_name}><Play_Info>GetParam</Play_Info></{src_name}>".format(self.source)
```

Then calling code would look like:

```
resp = self._request(PlayGet(source))
```

We could have a PlayGetResponse class that's responsible for the parsing (and also a good place in the code to document what the return document is going to look like (I've been writing some notes locally, but a standard place to do that in code would be nice).

That takes a bunch of internal complexity out of the methods in the main class, and makes it more of high level flow:

```
def do_thing(self):
    if not can_do_thing(self.input):
        bail
    resp = self._request(Thing(self.input))
    return resp
```

2) move static discovery into a dedicated module, though I'm not sure I really have a good handle on the exposed interface because we both want it for safety checks in our methods, but we are also going to want a `dump` or something to support systems like HomeAssistant setting the capabilities on the receiver when that interface is active (which impacts the controls shown to the user).

I prefer to bind a loopback interface 127.0.0.0/8 (according to the RFC 1700) and always keep the same port. It works on Linux, Windows (loopback adapter) and MacOS

On MacOS you must declare the new loopback interface manually like
`sudo ifconfig lo0 alias 127.6.6.6`
and to remove it
`sudo ifconfig lo0 -alias 127.6.6.6`

I use this tips to launch multiple versions of PostgreSQL server with loopback that containt the version number in the loopback address and port 5432 like:

- 127.0.9.3 for PostgreSQL 9.3
- 127.0.9.4 for PostgreSQL 9.4
- 127.0.9.5 for PostgreSQL 9.5

I use [SublimeCodeIntel](https://github.com/SublimeCodeIntel/SublimeCodeIntel) for coding assistance, and the dynamic nature of the lookup mechanism makes any sort of method lookup completely useless. This, combined with the fact that the information that you can subscribe to any IRC method in the `events.py` file by prefixing it with `on_` is barely documented, makes using the library a bit confusing.

Original comment by: Fake Name

> Do you mind if I also clean some code for the non-async part along the way if I happen to find any?

In general, this sort of contribution should be handled as a separate PR to be accepted first. You can still implement your asyncio improvements on top of these incidental changes, but then they'll be dependent on the acceptance of those changes and might require resolving of merge conflicts if not accepted exactly as presented. But this project is happy to accept all sorts of changes as long as the concerns can be kept separate. If the changes are small and non-controversial, it's fine to implement those in the same PR, just use separate commits (same guidance, just more lightweight).

> E.g. in connection.py, identity function could be replaced by lambda x: x.

You'll find the linter won't accept `identity = lambda x: x` (although that would have been my stylistic preference as well). You _could_ move the change inline (i.e. `wrapper=lambda x: x`), but that conveys less information.

Honestly, if I were you, I'd try to look past these stylistic changes and focus on the semantic or structural changes. But again, happy to review any proposed change.

> I think it's easier to work on a fork and make another package that only supports async irc code and release separately. What do you think?

A fork, especially in a different package/module, is just asking for trouble. It means creating two lines of development on a project that already has very little development.

I do see the appeal - that maintaining simple socket compatibility is hard - hence why we have something of a fork already with two implementations. I'd rather not increase the divergence with yet another package. It seems reasonable at first until we get the next bug report that affects the common parts of the two libraries - then we have to expend extra effort to ensure that both implementations get that functionality (or otherwise manage the increasing cognitive distance between the two projects).

Ideally, we could refactor the code in such a way that any event loop / IO handler could be chosen, and the rest of the functionality lies atop that, although I suspect that's easier said than done (if possible).

If a proper loop abstraction isn't possible or achievable, I'd be more inclined to drop support for Python 2 and a select loop, and rely entirely on async IO and Python 3 for the library (a backward-incompatible release).

So in that sense, I do support a fork, one in which the select-based functionality is left behind in a maintenance branch that's unlikely to get backports of functionality from the master. If you can bring the asyncio functionality to feature parity with the select loop, then I'm happy to accept that as the sole event loop for the library.

To the extent that you can, try to implement the changes with many minimal steps with stable commits.

Thanks!

use this one https://esi.tech.ccp.is/ui/?version=_latest (`get__latest_swagger`): it's the same as latest, but shows version instead of "latest", so you know what you use, and you can fix it after :) 

About LB, that's fun, i was sure I already changed it... but nop. About best practice, I can't recommend anything, but what i'd do (and what I thought I committed for LB) is to use each version, either by `esiapp.get_vX_swagger.op['somethingsomething']` or by saving them all and using the "shortcut" (as I do in LB).

For versions, yes, each version only shows endpoints that have that version, so your best bet is to find the latest on `_latest` then fix it in the code to use the given version. In the latest, you can't find the version yes, unless it's in the description, also the warning won't ever show if you use latest, as it only show for versions within "legacy" (so not anymore in "latest"). 

You'd better fix the version, check for warning, and update your code if there's a warning :)

@zodiac911 对，这的确是BUG  `import boxx.{操作}` 这个方式非常 trick 而且 Python2 和 Python3 结构上的不同导致 实现方式不同，效果也不同

在 Python 2 里面过 `import boxx.{操作}` 再 `from boxx import {操作}`是没有问题的， 但是  `import boxx.{操作}` 只能运行第一次

在 Python 3 里面  `import boxx.{操作}` 每次都能运行，但是再执行 `from boxx import {操作}`就被污染了

所以 tutorial 里面推荐 在不打算导入 `import boxx` 时才用 `import boxx.{操作}`

或者先执行 `from boxx import {操作}` 再执行  `import boxx.{操作}` 也 OK

```vim
let _foo = "I'm a private variable and not used, but matched by ^_"
```

So, I think we should limit the variable type that `ignored_pattern: '^_'` applied. Then the variable type might be only function/lambda arguments because unuvoidable unused variables are typically function/lambda arguments.

You can use the `Lambda` augmenter, which allows you to apply custom functions to images. A code example is below (untested). If that is not enough for your use case, you can take a look at `Fliplr` (horizontal flips) in `imgaug/augmenters/flip.py`. Its code is fairly easy to understand.

```python
backgrounds = np.random.randint(0, 256, size=(100, 128, 128, 3))

def func_images(images, random_state, parents, hooks):
    bg_ids = random_state.randint(0, len(backgrounds), size=(len(images),))
    result = []
    for image, bg_id in zip(images, bg_ids):
        image_small = ia.imresize_single_image(image, (64, 64))
        image_aug = np.copy(backgrounds[bg_id])
        image_aug[32:-32, 32:-32, :] = image_small
        result.append(image_aug)
    return result
    
def func_heatmaps(heatmaps, random_state, parents, hooks):
    return heatmaps
    
def func_keypoints(keypoints_on_images, random_state, parents, hooks):
    return keypoints_on_images

bg_augmenter = iaa.Lambda(
    func_images=func_images,
    func_heatmaps=func_heatmaps,
    func_keypoints=func_keypoints
)
seq = iaa.Sequential([
    iaa.Fliplr(0.5),
    bg_augmenter
])
```

@malinoff As @njsmith said, h2 isn't prone to those bugs because it doesn't interact with those systems. It can't fail to propagate backpressure because it doesn't own the source of bytes: the asyncio glue code does. h2 *will* buffer, if you ask it to, but it doesn't have to buffer any more than one complete HTTP/2 frame, because again the caller is responsible for feeding in the bytes and triggering the consumption of them. And finally, because h2 owns no sockets it cannot have problems with closing the event loop before the socket is drained.

All of these problems are in asyncio and the asyncio glue code. A trio/curio adapter to h2 would happily avoid them. There is no need for callbacks: all callbacks are doing is providing you with some implicit state management. By all means feel free to use Future objects, they're a perfectly cromulent abstraction, but your AMQP library could quite easily avoid both them and callbacks if you wanted.

`lego.equivalent` first converts the two `lego` objects to finite state machines, which is potentially very time-consuming when large numbers of possible characters are involved. It then computes the symmetric difference of the two FSMs, also potentially time-consuming, and finally checks that the result is empty, which is effectively instantaneous. However, in this case, I find that `parse('00|1').equivalent(parse('0(0|1)'))` returns `False` pretty much instantly.

Someone contributed the install target.  I resisted it for years because I
know most users don't understand the issues surrounding C++ ABI
portability, dynamic linking, the C++ one definition rule, and so forth.
That leads to confusing compiler/linker/runtime errors which then get
emailed to me, even though they have nothing to do with dlib.  However, on
unix platforms the install target is a sensible thing to have since unix
has a sensible way of dealing with shared libraries.  It's still a complex
issue, but isn't full of remotely as many hidden bombs as visual studio's
DLL mechanism.

My general advice is still to build everything together since the dlib
built time is not significant and doing so avoids any possibility of mixing
incompatibly compiled object files. This issue of mixing files compiled in
different and incompatible ways is far and away the number one complaint
people have about building C++ projects.  But if you compile everything at
once it's a non-issue since you have consistent compiler settings across
the entire build.  That kind of build system is very easy to get right,
even for novice users.  However, builds consisting of multiple
independently compiled object files/static/shared libraries require
somewhat deep C++ expertise and understanding of your compiler to get
right.  The advantage is *sometimes* faster compiles. However, there are a
lot of ways to speed up a build that are much easier to get right.

Exporting maps with a drawing layer to PNG should just work -- I'll open a separate issue. Note that the user won't be able to interact with the PNG, so presumably that won't solve your issue?

With the package as it currently stands, exporting an interactive version of the drawing layer to HTML is not possible -- user interactions in the drawing layer rely on the maps being backed by a Jupyter server. I agree that it would be nice to move that into the browser client so it still works when exported to HTML.

If I were in your position, I would either:

- host a notebook server on a cloud provider (e.g. Amazon EC2) and invite *trusted* users to draw on that directly. If you do that, you need to make sure you secure access to your notebook.
- with untrused users, use very experimental widget dashboarding features like [this](https://github.com/pbugnion/ipywidgets_server) or [this](https://github.com/maartenbreddels/flask-ipywidgets) to host a dashboard on a cloud provider (e.g. Amazon EC2).
- use something like [binder](https://mybinder.org/) to provide a throw-away notebook environment backed by a GitHub repository.
- use the free trial period of a platform for hosting notebooks -- [Google Colaboratory](https://colab.research.google.com/notebooks/welcome.ipynb) (I have no experience with that, so no promises) or [SherlockML](https://sherlockml.com/) (full disclosure: this is what I work on for my day job).

Please not that in early versions of typing.py module the `Type` symbol does not exist. Could you please make sure that it is not imported by enforce ? Otherwise new versions would break on our production environment (python 3.5.1, with no possibility to update the typing module using pip).

In all of my code to cope with this restriction I always quote `Type` when used in type hints, and add the following import: 

```python
try:
    from typing import Type
except ImportError:
    # normal for old versions of typing
    pass
```

Of course in your case you do want to reason about a type hint being the `Type` symbol, so I suggest the following import trick:

```python
```python
try:
    from typing import Type
except ImportError:
    # normal for old versions of typing
    class Type:
        pass
```

I've changed how the offset computation is performed in commit bb43726ad64948a2038a03590d7b5d312f9452b7. The previous implementation was trying to be smart about the shapes but it lacked generality. The algorithm now should be general and more mathematically rigorous with the definition of offset.
The price for that is the computational cost, which has **increased significantly** due to the new rounded corners, whereas before they were single points.
Also note that similar to `boolean`, `offset` also has an `eps` parameter that can help in case of segfaults.

@rosivda's example now gives the same output in both cases for positive offsets (expansion). Negative offsets (shrinking) take into account internal boundaries, and that is by design. If you don't want internal boundaries showing up in the result, you have to merge all polygons beforehand. Merging the geometry is a good idea for expansion as well, since it reduces the size of the problem.

Please let me know if the new `offset` works and I'll make an official release.

In case anyone is wondering about the "file-notify" method, I got it to work using the same `base16-wal-theme.el` file as above, but put this in my `.emacs` instead:

```
(defun refresh-theme ()
  (progn
    (load-theme 'base16-wal t)))

(defun theme-callback (event)
  (refresh-theme))

(require 'filenotify)
(file-notify-add-watch
  "/home/paul/.emacs.d/base16-wal-theme.el" '(change) 'theme-callback)
```

Essentially, you define the same `refresh-theme` variable above, but use another variable `theme-callback` to satisfy the callback argument for `file-notify`.

Adding in the template functionality from `pywal`, theme switching for `emacs` is now 100% automated by adding a template file called `base16-wal-theme.el` in `~/.config/wal/templates/` 

You can then use the command `wal -g -i /path/to/wallpapers && cp /home/${USER}/.cache/wal/base16-wal-theme.el /home/${USER}/.emacs.d/base16-wal-theme.el` to apply the new theme.  It takes about an extra second, but it's still about as "on the fly" as you can get.  Hope this helps someone!

Data is split sequentially based on the indexing at the start. So in general, they will share the same split (unless the number of folds is varied, but there would still be an overlap). One way around this would be to shuffle the input data between layers.

Currently, if ``shuffle=True``, the input is shuffled at the ensemble level before any calls to the ``ParallelManager``. To prevent sharing splits, shuffling inputs should be performed between layers, so the it needs to be moved in to the ``ParallelManager.process`` method. Shouldn't be too hard though.

That is the intended behaviour. Since a hidden layer or a meta learner is trained on the predictions of the previous layer, each layer must be configured to predict probabilities or not at initialisation (i.e. in the ``add`` method).

Exposing a ``predict_proba`` method is just for compatibility with the scikit-learn API.

Hi,

I've thought about it some more, and would like to propose an alternate design, since I see some problems with the current design:

1. `get_reactor()` is additional burden on the user.
2. `get_reactor()` is error-prone. The common Twisted idiom is "store reference to reactor", and if you do that pre-fork you will get confusing results.
3. The destruction of pre-fork reactor presumes it's reasonable to run code in the reactor before fork. Pre-fork is supposed to be just setup, though, all business logic should only run post-fork.

My proposal:

Add a new API called `crochet.prefork_setup()`, to be used in parent pre-fork process. It will install an unusable reactor object: if code uses a reactor API with this object an error is raised. This ensures no reactor code is run pre-fork. `prefork_setup()` also stores the process PID.

If `crochet.setup()` or `crochet.no_setup()` are called and the current PID doesn't match stored PID, they know they are now in post-fork setup mode. That means normal crochet setup, but also replacing `twisted.internet.reactor` with the real reactor (by changing original object's class and `__dict__` to that of the newly created real reactor).

This is pretty awful, but so is much of Crochet... but it's hidden awfulness that should make for a simpler and easier to use API.

The documentation would just say "call `crochet.prefork_setup()` in original process, call `crochet.setup()` after forking. note you can't use reactor in original process.".

Does that make sense?

@feranto63, now I have a few more suggestions.

1. Try setting `retries` for `PoolManager`. By default, it does not retry. Below, I tell it to retry 3 times. Put this segment *at the beginning* of your program.
    ```python
    import telepot.api

    telepot.api._pools = {
        'default': urllib3.PoolManager(num_pools=3, maxsize=10, retries=3, timeout=30),
    }
    ```

2. Override function `_which_pool` to force independent connection each time:
    ```python
    def force_independent_connection(req, **user_kw):
        return None

    telepot.api._which_pool = force_independent_connection
    ```

3. Set `retries` even for independent connections:
    ```python
    telepot.api._onetime_pool_spec = (urllib3.PoolManager, dict(num_pools=1, maxsize=1, retries=3, timeout=30))
    ```

Try them in order, and see which one (or combination of them) can remedy your situation.

This is correct behavior and documented in the [README](https://github.com/phihag/ipaddress/blob/master/README.md). If you forget `from __future__ import unicode_literals`, your code is equivalent to `ipaddress.ip_address(b'blah')`. `b'blah'` is the so-called [packed](https://docs.python.org/dev/library/ipaddress.html#ipaddress.IPv4Address.packed) representation of an IP address.

Under Python 3, `ipaddress.ip_address(b'blah') == ipaddress.IPv4Address(u'98.108.97.104')`. Since this backport aims to be compatible (no sense in writing *new* code that fails under Python 3), we are locked into this behavior.

If you want to create an ip_address from a human-readable representation instead from a byte string, pass in a character string instead of a byte string. For string literals, either put `from __future__ import unicode_literals` to the top of your file, or prefix character strings with `u`. For other inputs, [decode](https://docs.python.org/dev/library/stdtypes.html#bytes.decode) them from a byte string.

What appears to be happening here is that you are bouncing between group coordinators and your consumer is resetting to the last committed offset, which in your case is nothing so you reset to the beginning.

A bit of background: we're generating [PDF of Jupyter Notebooks](https://github.com/Anaconda-Server/nbbrowserpdf) with ghost, and it's pretty good. The issue that's tripping me up for a class of generation is in connecting to pages that use WebSockets, and a number of special flags, etc. need to be set, etc... so it might be more of an inconvenience than a requirement, as I haven't full explored that space. However, as I [continue testing](https://github.com/Anaconda-Server/nbbrowserpdf/issues/4), I imagine I'll encounter limitations of QtWebKit, whether javascript, CSS or just craziness, as the pages I am rendering can indeed get pretty crazy.

Having done some more research on the matter: apparently, QWebKit hasn't been updated for some time. Thus, getting ghost to work with it's latest version would be nice, if easy, as we would get to the last state of improvement webkit is likely to get. A high-value goal for emulating desktop browsers even more closely would be the Qt5-only QtWebEngine, which is basically chromium. Among other things, you can connect to it from the [chrome dev tools](https://github.com/PySide/pyside2/wiki/Inspecting-QWebEngineViews) which is magic.

QtWebEngine is not supported by pyqt, but there is at least [one example](https://github.com/rodrigogolive/WebEngineWrapper) of using it via round-about methods... and may not expose all of the APIs ghost would need.

[pyside2](https://github.com/PySide/pyside2/tree/master/PySide2/QtWebEngineWidgets) does have support for QtWebEngine, but [it's not ready yet](http://it.toolbox.com/blogs/enlightenment/pyside2-pyside-qt5-support-underway-68285).

So if really significant work is required to get ghost to work with qt5, it may be worth waiting until one of the upstreams supports QtWebEngine to invest significant work. Of the two possible upstreams, pyside is the one that would actually accept effort, as I couldn't even find the code for pyqt in non-dump-from.

I believe this is because you didn't anchor the directories at the root directory. So `js` and `css` directories inside `/dist` will be excluded. Try the following. The leading slash indicates the file is anchored at the root of the repository.

```
"ignore": [
    "/node_modules",
    "/bower_components",
    "/.git",
    "/addons",
    "/beta-testing",
    "/css",
    "/docs",
    "/js",
    "/testing",
    "/Gruntfile.js",
    "/package.json",
    "*.txt",
    "*.md",
    "*.html",
    "/tablesorter.jquery.json"
]
```

1. ``None`` - no replacements are made, even of the value defined by the NULL line in the ~W section. This behaviour would not conform to the specification but I can see it as potentially useful.
2. ``"NULL"`` - the only replacement made is the value defined by the NULL line in the ~W section. This would conform to the specification I think.
3. ``"common"`` - replacements would include NULL, (-)(9)999.(25)(0), 1.#IND, 1.#INF, -1.#IO, #N/A, etc., (+|-)2147483647, (+|-)32767, etc.
4. ``"aggressive"`` - replacements would include all those defined by ``"common"``, plus algorithms like that I suggest in the original post.

The default would be either ``"NULL"`` or ``"common"`` -- I would prefer the latter.

@jamiebull1 , Your comments on testing are right on the ball.

I am setting up some general direction on testing here. Some of it is driven by having to maintain clarity. This clarity is important especially in communication to other developers who may come on board

So the present thinking on unit testing
- use unit tests (pytest) when there are no external dependencies
- use integration tests when there are external dependencies

known external dependencies:
- IO operations (read and write to disk)
- running Energyplus from eppy
- find location of Energyplus (preferably latest Energyplus)
- find location of IDD file

Right now eppy does not have any integration tests. We can jury rig something for the moment. For the long term, some thought needs to be put into this.

Function with IO operations can be tested without invoking the IO operations.
For the moment let us do the following for functions with IO operations
.- write the function so that it can take the filehandle or file name as an argument
- while unit testing, use the filehandle
- don't test with filenames in unit tests

rewritten function and test may look like this (reiterating what was in the previous post) :

    def changetxt_orig(fname, change):
        txt = open(fname, 'r').read()
        return change + txt
        
    def changetxt_alter(fname, change):
        try:
            txt = open(fname, 'r').read()
        except TypeError as e:
            # it is not a file. Assume it is a file handle
            fhandle = fname
            txt = fhandle.read()
        return change + txt

    def test_changetxt_alter():
        origtxt = "spam"
        change = "not "
        newtxt = "not spam"
        import StringIO
        filehandle = StringIO.StringIO(origtxt)
        result = changetxt_alter(filehandle, change)
        assert result == newtxt
        
Some of the eppy test need large text snippets. Keeping these text snippets in the test modules will clutter it up. `snippet.py` is a good place to keep them. The idd file is extremely large. The iddfile can be imported from `iddcurrent.py`

I am still researching how to set up a process for doing integration tests. At least looking at how others do it. Will write further on it once I have some clarity. @jamiebull1  if you have any insight into this, I would like to hear

Next steps:
- update tests for functions with IO operations so that they use filehandles
- for the moment we will not have tests that write to the disk.

Hopefully I'll quickly come up with a strategy for doing integration tests

Darren, Jamie,

Thank you for your responses. This has been a very encouraging discussion. I have been traveling with a lot of down time. So I had time to think about this

But first let me respond to your comments.

Darren, I agree that eppy would use the lower level API. We can decide whether it should be embedded or imported. When the time comes, we can follow licensing requirements. (You may have to remind me)

Jamie, `bunch` may be the easiest way to get to the dot notation that eppy uses, but I am not wedded to it. (`bunch` overrides `__getattr__` for you) Regarding the larger program structure, I am keeping it vague for now, mostly so that I don't get mentally locked into one way of seeing things. Your description is a reasonable take on it


Coming back to the topic of results and outputs. Some of the E+ outputs are:
- HTML tables
- csv files
- sql files
- eso files
- rdd files

I see results module broken up in the following way:
- reader
- wrapper

The "reader" reads the output data into a python data structure. There will be some fairly straight forward coding here, using appropriate library (sqlite3, csv, beautifulsoup etc). Some smart thinking will have to be done while designing the python data structure. The "reader" may be embedded in eppy or maybe an external package that is imported. At this point, even without the "wrapper", we have a useable results module. 

The "wrapper" wraps it into a dot format that eppy uses. 

I am drawn into this way of structuring, because of the input API of eppy is written this way. [Underlying Data Structure of eppy](http://pythonhosted.org/eppy/dev_docs/data_eppy.html) describes some of this. This was a historic accident rather than intentional, but worked out pretty well. 

I have a couple of motivations for using esoreader in eppy
- I would like to see an "ecosystem" of open source software that support each other in the energy modeling world.
- using esoreader in eppy will encourage and support Darren in his development of esoreader.
- over time we may develop a protocol for how the packages in the "ecosystem" work with each other. This may just be some informal rules. 

I am saying this without looking deep into esoreader code. From Jamie's and Darren's responses, it looks like esoreader is a viable candidate for eppy to use.

---------------------

#### Other ideas
eppy will be significantly more powerful with the following api call

    idf.run()
    
This will basically run the idf file. So we can do things like:

    # untested code
    def reduce_cooling():
        """reduce cooling by increasing SHGC"""
        if idf.results.cooling > tippingpoint_c:
            reached_tippingpoint = False
            for window in largewindows:
                window.construction = improveSHGC(window.construction)
                # improveSHGC will improve the SHGC in the construction
        else:
            reached_timmpingpoint = True
        return reached_timmpingpoint
    
    reached_timmpingpoint = False
    idf.run()
    while not reached_timmpingpoint:
        reached_timmpingpoint = reduce_cooling()
        if reached_timmpingpoint:
            break
        else:
            idf.run()

I know there are other tools that will let you do something similar, but this single function `idf.run()` can really change the nature of eppy.

What is interesting is that `idf.run()` should be configurable so that:
- It can run locally on your machine
- can run on a remote machine
- can run on the cloud
- can be run by a third party service ( [apidae ?](https://apidaelabs.com) )

At present eppy does not know that a run is completed. E+ should be able to send a signal back to eppy so that the loop can continue.

hmmm i do not think this is the right approach.

this change makes the pool always close a connection when a connection is returned. this is contrary to the whole point of the pool, which is connection reuse. with this proposed change the pool would degrade to the equivalent of "open, use, close", which can also be achieved without using a pool in the first place,

that said, i think the right approach is to create the pool _after_ forking, since connections are process-local.

also, a pool only makes sense for multithreaded (or green threaded) apps, multiprocessing does not do that so you could also make a connection directly.  the only benefit of the pool would be connection recovery in case of failure.

@Akuli Converting to GIF can have unintended consequences for some themes because the color space is more limited. It does support transparency though (at least with Tk >8.5, I'm not sure about 8.4).

Conversion would be a pretty big undertaking though. Conversion with PIL is not sufficient, because transparency conversion does not work quite correctly:
![screenshot](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/15170036/39958211-21ea980c-5600-11e8-9984-1e005d535bc1.png)

Conversion with `imagemagick` can work though:
![screenshot](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/15170036/39958369-427d39ce-5602-11e8-8954-acbe68c31a56.png)
Though in at least a few images conversion artifacts occur because those images do not only make uses of alpha pixels, but also the alpha channel in order to make for soft edges. They would have to be fixed by hand where possible, and the result would not be nearly as good as it is right now. Also, that would mean dynamic themeing would only be possible if `tkimg` is present, because that would require using PNG images still.

It would also mean that introducing new themes would be more complicated as well. I was hoping to port over a few more Gtk-themes like I did with `equilux`(#29 ), even though it's not completely done yet.

Considering everything, I will not convert the PNG-based themes to GIF-only images just yet. I first want to see if more people continue having issues. In the meantime, I will look into the possibility of having the package support an option for installing `tkimg` support at runtime, possibly by building a separate package to include `tkimg`.

First, thanks for your dedication into writing `natsort` and all its documentation. It is really hard to find someone so dedicated maintaining (on their free time) good open source software.

> I had spent over a week (nights and weekends) writing the How it Works page in the docs in the hopes that users of the library would read it and have an understanding of how natsort works so that they could debug issues that arise or learn how to hack the algorithm.

When I first got the `TypeError`, I was interested into figuring out why my code was breaking `natsort`, because:

1. Either I wrote something wrong and I needed to fix so my code can be robust. 
1. Or because there was a bug in the `natsort` library, and I would like to report the bug.

The first thing which bothered me was the fact that the builtin python `sorted()` function was working fine. Next to it, I tried to reproduce the bug outside my system, but `natsort` worked fine with everything I throwed at it.


> help by suggesting how that page can be improved or how the README could be improved so that what was going on and how to solve the issue would be more clear.

The README page could always use show the use of `key` when calling `natsort` on this examples, even when they are already native strings. With that, I would figured out sooner that with `natsort` I should usually set what is the comparison attribute key.

After understanding how `natsort` works, I think there is nothing much which could be done:

1. Either mention this special behavior on the README when comparing custom objects, 
1. Or perhaps, if no key is provided by the user, then set by default the key as `str`.
1. Or always use tuples when `natsort` generates its keys. Therefore, there is no way this error can happen again as everything would always being tuples.
   I am not sure about the performance implication of this or if such thing would be possible. Some bench marking should be done, and see if this changes the library performance, or if it is irrelevant.
   If this impact the performance, then just documenting this behavior on README should be enough.

The reason that buttons are represented as _enums_ is because of the simplicity of consumption: a simple `if button == Button.left` is enough to check which button was pressed.

At the same time, I realise that the library breaking for arbitrary buttons is... well, bad. Unfortunately your proposed solution does not work; it will modify `Button.unknown.button_number` every time an unknown button is encountered, leading to strange behaviour in multi threaded code.

At the moment, the best solution I can think of is extending [5bddf97 - Added values for button 8 and 9 for Xorg](https://github.com/moses-palmer/pynput/commit/5bddf97e3d42fae042f865634b3ea53491ae81f2) to include even more buttons. While this solution may seem less elegant, there does exist some limitation on the possible number of buttons, so it might not be as bad as it seems.

Another possibility is to create a different, non-enum, class for buttons greater than `Button.button9`. What do you think? I have never used a mouse with more than seven buttons, including the scroll wheel, so I might not be the best judge ;-)

**No, that is not what I am not saying at all!** Bug fixes are fine, I just don't think I will be patching older versions of the library, as I don't see how it would ever be necessary. Patches are for when you cut a release of something (say 1.0.0) and development continues (say we have just released 1.1.0) but a user has an issue on the older (1.0.0) version and is unable to upgrade. Then you would checkout the older minor version and issue a patch (ie 1.0.1) with a bugfix. I am assuming here that the issue does not occur in a newer version (say 1.1.0 or newer) and that something else is preventing the user from upgrading to 1.1.0 or newer. Unless I am mistaken in what a patch is, what I am claiming is that this is a highly unlikely scenario for the users of spiceypy, largely because I don't know why upgrading would not be an option. And again as I said, using semantic versioning resolves the issue as I would then be able to issue a patch, no matter how unlikely it is.

I feel like this can be solved reasonably well with a custom data structure. I'm in the middle of something, but I have a thing in mind which I think will do it. I'll need to get back to you later with details though.

Intersection (give me all numbers whose codepoint is in this range) is definitely the correct behaviour. It's both necessary for the use case of "MySQL can't handle codepoints high enough to require 4 bytes in utf-8" and also seems like the one that makes most logical sense.

One complication is that this can result in an empty set of valid code points. If I ask for all surrogates in the range [0, 127] this has no valid characters. This should raise an InvalidArgument error (I think it's fine if any individual category misses the range, just not if all of them do).

Depending on the representation of our precomputed table and/or the size of the range this might be easy or hard to detect, which is why I thought it worth mentioning here. You definitely should not feel in any way obliged to do this unless you actually want to.

That error is purposefully thrown into the log as an Exception with traceback the first time it occurs while reconnecting. After the first failed attempt, the message will be thrown at the Debug level so as not to spam the log. It is an important message, so it is best not to silence it completely. The message will reappear in the log if it is raised the next time PyChromecast is reconnecting.

See: https://github.com/balloob/pychromecast/blob/bbcf72c3618a31a306ce85e4b7678b59277113b7/pychromecast/socket_client.py#L235

> What do you think? Do you buy this argument?

In general, I think we should be following the macaroon algorithms specified in the paper.

Here's the relevant snippet from the paper. Here M is the root macaroon and 𝓜 is the set of discharge macaroons. Note that it uses the same M for all discharge macaroons, and that *all* discharge macaroons are in 𝓜, not just the discharges for the top level macaroon.

    M.PrepareForRequest(𝓜)
        𝓜sealed := ∅
        for M' ∈ 𝓜
            macaroon@L'<id', C' , sig'> ← M'
            sig'' := M.bindForRequest(sig')
            𝓜sealed := 𝓜sealed ∪ {macaroon@L'<id' , C' , sig''>}
        return 𝓜sealed

This is borne out by the way that Verify is specified. Note that even though Verify is called recursively, the same TM is used throughout (this is the difference from the current pymacaroons behaviour).

    M.Verify(TM , k, 𝒜, 𝓜)
        cSig := MAC(k, M.id )
        for i := 0 to |M.C| − 1
            ...
            assert (∃M' ∈ 𝓜 : M'.id = cId ∧ M'.Verify(TM, cK, 𝒜, 𝓜))
            ...
        assert (M.sig = TM.bindForRequest(cSig))
        return true

Here's the paper describing how Verify is used:

> In order to verify an incoming access request consisting of an authorizing macaroon TM and a set of discharge macaroons 𝓜, a target service must ensure that all first-party and third-party caveats embedded in either TM or any macaroon in 𝓜 are satisfied.
> [...]
> To authorize the request, the target service invokes the method TM.Verify(TM, k, 𝒜, 𝓜) where k is the root key of macaroon TM.

Note that 𝓜 contains *all* the discharge macaroons, not just the discharge macaroons for the root macaroon, and observe that the same root signature is used to bind all those macaroons, because the same TM is used for all calls to bindForRequest, regardless of the level of recursion.

I don't really want to get into discussing whether this behaviour is correct or not from a security point of view; I am not a security expert - I trust the intuition and proofs of the paper's authors, and I don't think we should deviate from the algorithms specified in the paper lightly. If we do, we're running the risk of creating something that is not secure.

> Also, is there some property of binding all of the discharges to the root that you're relying on for a particular application?

It's crucial that all implementations do the binding in the same way, otherwise macaroon implementations are not interchangeable.

I'm using djangoUrl.reverse() as described and it works perfectly, however in certain instances I'd like the browser location to reflect the *actual* URI, not the djangoUrl()'ified URI. In other words, when the user clicks on an ```<a>``` tag whose ng-href is dynamically set by djangoUrl.reverse(), the browser shows

    http://someserver.com/angular/reverse/?djng_url_name=foo:bar&djng_url_kwarg_slug=quux-123

instead of the app URI:

    http://someserver.com/foo/bar/quux-123/

The latter is desirable for several reasons:

* From the point-of-view of Cool URIs Don't Change  - consider an app where several points may direct the user to the same page - some via angular, and others with URIs simply encoded by django. Now there are two URIs for the same page. If the user bookmarks the django_angular form, and then django_angular is replaced by some other technology, the bookmarked URI may no longer be valid.

* The django_angular URI exposes information about the nature and implementation of the app that might provide an attacker with ammunition - it is plainly obvious from just the browser's location bar that the server is running django, and how URLs are namespaced.

* The app URI is immediately associated with my app, instead of looking a little like some remote service. This could be important from the viewpoint of a user who may become suspicious that their session is being hijacked.

* It just looks cleaner.

This can be trivially solved by using an HTTP redirect in the middleware instead of calling the view directly (this will also have additional side-benefits - requesting the same URI once if a 301 redirect is used, e.g). However, from what I've seen, and the discussion above, this might not be desirable for some reason. Can you explain the reasons a redirect is not being used? Since the middleware has all the information it needs to construct a redirect, the fix might be as simple as:

    return HttpResponseRedirect(urls.reverse(...))

instead of the current

    new_request = WSGIRequest(request.environ)
    request.__dict__ = new_request.__dict__

I have pushed some changes that allow creating the engine in a new process group, so it will survive a keyboard interrupt.

Then it becomes pretty straight forward, like you are planning in method 2.

```python
import chess.uci
import os

engine = chess.uci.Engine(lambda engine: chess.uci.PopenProcess(engine, "stockfish", preexec_fn=os.setpgrp))

try:
    print("go:", engine.go(depth=32))
except KeyboardInterrupt:
    print("stopping ...")
    engine.stop()
    print("stopped.")

print("alive:", engine.is_alive())

print("second go:", engine.go(depth=10))
```

Unfortunately due to a limition in the backport of `concurrent.futures` this will not work in Python 2. (The engine process will live, but the wait on `engine.go()` will keep blocking).

Hi @johnduxx I'm glad you're interested in contributing to this project. To answer your question, ChatterBot uses a number of algorithms to select a response. ChatterBot is designed to be modular so each response selection algorithm is broken up into its own module called a logic adapter. A number of logic adapters have been written, at the moment most of them implement search-based artificial intelligence to select an appropriate response to the user's input. Bayesian networks are also used but to a limited extent.

I have been researching seq-to-seq machine learning methods using technologies such as TensorFlow, maybe in the future ChatterBot will have these capabilities as well.

Let me know if you have any further questions 😃

Chances are it's an environmental issue when you run in AWS Lambda (which is the purpose of Zappa if I'm not mistaken) since it needs to install dependencies. Double check your `requirements.txt` file or however you're having the AWS Lambda system setup your Python environment. 

You might wan to check if that Lambda instance is properly installing the foreign function interface module (`ffi`) per [this SO answer](https://raspberrypi.stackexchange.com/a/28189) to a related traceback. Maybe try adding `libffi-dev` to your `requirements.txt` since you won't have access to `apt`.

Ultimately Lambda is just using an underlying EC2 instance, so you could also spin up your own EC2 system using the base Amazon Linux AMI that Lambda runs your code on and make sure things work for you there. See http://docs.aws.amazon.com/lambda/latest/dg/current-supported-versions.html

Wow. Great catch, and a great point.

This is also the case in all of the other usages of try_match, i.e.

1. Matching origins ('foo' will match 'foo-hijacker').
2. Matching resource paths ('/foo' will match '/foo-private')
3. Headers, as you mentioned.

I think a better way to fix this is to make try_match smarter, by having it inspect if the string is likely a regex, and if so, use a regex match

I.E.

```Python
def try_match(request_origin, pattern):
    """Safely attempts to match a pattern or string to a request origin."""
    if probably_regex(pattern):
            return re.match(pattern, request_origin, flags=re.IGNORECASE)
    else:
        return request_origin == pattern
```

What do you think about that solution? I hate that we are using a heuristic for whether or not something is a regex, but I think if we do it this way, at least we are consistent.

It'd be great to add a few tests around this, if you have some spare time, if not I'll hope to get to it this weekend.

```
from cryptography.hazmat.backends import default_backend
from cryptography.x509 import load_pem_x509_certificate

key = load_pem_x509_certificate(self.setting('ID_TOKEN_DECRYPTION_KEY'), default_backend())
public_key = key.public_key()
decode_kwargs = {
    'algorithms': ['RS256'],
    'audience': client_id,
    'issuer': self.ID_TOKEN_ISSUER,
    'key': public_key,
    'options': {
        'verify_signature': True,
        'verify_exp': True,
        'verify_iat': True,
        'verify_aud': True,
        'verify_iss': True,
        'require_exp': True,
        'require_iat': True,
    },
}
```

def forward(self, to_recipients, cc_recipients=None, bcc_recipients=None):

The reason why this is happening is that `__next__()` is not defined for the `UnicodeReader`. Python 2 uses the `next()` method and Python 3 uses the `__next__()` magic method. Unfortunately, that's the least of the problems with `unicodecsv`'s `UnicodeReader` iterating in Python 3. The other problems are:
* the `self.reader.next()` method call (should be `next(self.reader)` in Python 3)
* the call to `unicode()`
* also, you'd need to use `io.StringIO()`, not `io.BytesIO()` in Python 3

The parser allows titles to be chained to recognize things like "vice president" and "secretary general". Because "dr" and "king" are both titles, the parser counts them both as titles. If we only give the parser one name we assume it's a last name, so because there's only on part that isn't a title, Jr. is treated as a last name. 

I think this is a limitation of a simple rule based approach. I tried messing with it but couldn't think of anything that wouldn't break other tests.

You can fix this in your code by removing "king" from the titles constant if "king" is not a common title in your dataset.

```
    >>> from nameparser.config import CONSTANTS
    >>> CONSTANTS.titles.remove('king')
```

of server-side URL generation and client-side code to upload directly to
S3. It's not compatible with the Django file storage save() API, which
assumes the server has a file object representing the file data.

You could write you own code to save the file in S3 directly, then access
it via django-s3-storage. This is a good article on how to do the upload
part:

(See the reference to the [CMS](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Chicago_Manual_of_Style) in my link above.) 

For the third link, section three indicates usage of apostrophes to "indicate the structure of unusual words" and I think this gets at why I do this. There are vetted examples of this general concept and, unless someone can point out otherwise, there are no formal rules for pluralizing programming class names in English. I do it because **WaveObjects** *could* be a valid class name (and therefore might be confusing to an unfamiliar reader), whereas **WaveObject's** would not be a valid class name in most programming languages so there's less ambiguity. Admittedly, it's probably a small difference, but one I find *useful*. 

As for the universality of English, I won't try to argue either way, but I will betray my bias by recommending Pinker's [The Language Instinct](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Language_Instinct) - one of the few books I've read but a good (and fun) one. Let's just say I find it squishy.

@jschneier I am also facing this same issue when trying to clear the S3 bucket before uploading files again. I looked into it and I believe I've found the cause of this problem, at least for my use case. My version of `django-storages` is 1.5.2. Django version is 1.11.

Here's what my S3 bucket looks like:
```
admin/css/style.css
images/logo.png
css/main.css
```

It has a number of files, but at the top-level all I have are folders. The problem is that the `collectstatic.clear_dir` command calls the `listdir(name='')` of the storage (`S3Boto3` in my case). Since the name is empty, the `listdir` command gets a list of *all* the files in the S3 bucket, and loops over them. But instead of adding the individual file names to the list of files, it only adds the directory names. So for the first invocation of `listdir(name='')` from `collectstatic`, the return value is: `dirs = ['admin', 'images', 'css'], files = []`. This happens in `s3boto3.py:490`.

This would be fine for a normal hierarchical filesystem, as in the next step the `collectstatic` command calls the `clear_dir` method with each of the directory names. However, with S3, which has no concept of a directory, the first step in `clear_dir`, which checks if the path exists, fails. On a normal file system this would succeed and the `clear_dir` would keep recursively calling itself until it would get to the lowest folder in the hierarchy containing just files, at which point those files would be deleted. But on S3, this failure means that if your S3 bucket has any folders, those will never be deleted.

I think the way to fix this would be to change the `S3Boto3.listdir` method to return just a list of files, as that is the view that S3 has of the bucket, instead of using the hierarchical view that most other file systems have. Once we return just a list of files and forget about directories (since in reality S3 doesn't have directories, and folders are just and illusion provided by the S3 console).

If you agree with this suggested way of fixing this problem, I'd be happy to make the changes and submit a pull request for your approval.

```
# add seed
def beta_geometric_nbd_model(T, r, alpha, a, b, size=1):
    if type(T) in [float, int]:
        T = T * np.ones(size)
    else:
        T = np.asarray(T)

    np.random.seed(188898)
    probability_of_post_purchase_death = stats.beta.rvs(a, b, size=size)
    np.random.seed(188898)
    lambda_ = stats.gamma.rvs(r, scale=1. / alpha, size=size)

    columns = ['frequency', 'recency', 'T', 'lambda', 'p', 'alive', 'customer_id']
    df = pd.DataFrame(np.zeros((size, len(columns))), columns=columns)

    for i in range(size):
        p = probability_of_post_purchase_death[i]
        l = lambda_[i]

        # hacky until I can find something better
        times = []
        np.random.seed(188898)
        next_purchase_in = stats.expon.rvs(scale=1. / l)
        alive = True
        while (np.sum(times) + next_purchase_in < T[i]) and alive:
            times.append(next_purchase_in)
            np.random.seed(188898)
            next_purchase_in = stats.expon.rvs(scale=1. / l)
            np.random.seed(188898)
            alive = np.random.random() > p

        times = np.array(times).cumsum()
        df.iloc[i] = len(times), np.max(times if times.shape[0] > 0 else 0), T[i], l, p, alive, i

    return df.set_index('customer_id')


def beta_geometric_nbd_model_transactional_data(T, r, alpha, a, b, start_date='2019-1-1', size=1):
    if type(T) in [float, int]:
        T = T * np.ones(size)
    else:
        T = np.asarray(T)

    start_date = pd.to_datetime(start_date)
    np.random.seed(188898)
    probability_of_post_purchase_death = stats.beta.rvs(a, b, size=size)
    np.random.seed(188898)
    lambda_ = stats.gamma.rvs(r, scale=1. / alpha, size=size)

    columns = ['customer_id', 'date']
    df = pd.DataFrame(columns=columns)

    for i in range(size):
        p = probability_of_post_purchase_death[i]
        l = lambda_[i]
        age = T[i]

        purchases = [[i, start_date - pd.DateOffset(days=1)]]  # where I change
        np.random.seed(188898)
        next_purchase_in = stats.expon.rvs(scale=1. / l)
        alive = True

        while next_purchase_in < age and alive:
            purchases.append([i, start_date + pd.DateOffset(days=next_purchase_in)])
            np.random.seed(188898)
            next_purchase_in += stats.expon.rvs(scale=1. / l)
            np.random.seed(188898)
            alive = np.random.random() > p

        df = df.append(pd.DataFrame(purchases, columns=columns))

    return df.reset_index(drop=True)
```

Not really. But I think I believe to know what the problem is.
In that naive implementation mentioned earlier, the token is refreshed on every request. On. Every. Request.  If you fire multiple requests at once, Django attempts to write as often to the database. I am under the impression that sqlite does not deal with this workload very well. Also, I dare to say that we don't need sub-second precision in the auth-token.
So I came up with the following which works reasonably well (well, so far at least):

```
class KnoxTokenAuthentication(TokenAuthentication):
    def authenticate(self, request):
        try:
            result = super().authenticate(request)
            if result:
                user, auth_token = result
                current_expiry = auth_token.expires
                new_expiry = timezone.now() + knox_settings.TOKEN_TTL
                auth_token.expires = new_expiry
                # This is only papering over sqlite's poor parallel write handling
                # Once we think we don't need sqlite any more, we can as well get rid
                # of this guard.
                if (new_expiry - current_expiry).total_seconds() > 1:
                    log.info("Expiry of token %r: %s  Updating to: %s", auth_token, current_expiry, new_expiry)
                    auth_token.save(update_fields=('expires',))
            return result
        except AuthenticationFailed:
            return None

```

You don't need celery to see what I was describing.

Celery has a function called `get_task_logger`. Among other things, it creates a new logger with a given name (like `getLogger`), and overrides the `.parent` before it hands it back, setting it to the logger for `celery.task`.

The special behavior is entirely having to do with overriding that `parent` attribute. From what I can tell, that's done primarily to allow the logging formatting to be configured globally on the `celery.task` logger, and I suspect there's some magic in that formatter so that it can figure out where the task is currently running.

-----

This is a minimal example so that you can see how things look when the `parent` is overridden:

```pycon
>>> import logging, logging_tree
>>> a = logging.getLogger('a')
>>> b = logging.getLogger('b')
>>> bcd = logging.getLogger('b.c.d')
>>> a.level = logging.DEBUG
>>> b.level = logging.INFO
>>> bcd.parent = a
>>> logging_tree.printout()
<--""
   Level WARNING
   |
   o<--"a"
   |   Level DEBUG
   |
   o<--"b"
       Level INFO
       |
       o<--[b.c]
           |
           o<--"b.c.d"
               Level NOTSET so inherits level DEBUG
```

FYI, to use `tex2jax` you have to use two options.  First you must ignore any element with any class and any element with no class.  Then, you provide an exception telling it to process any element with the `arithmatex` class.  That way you basically tell it to only process elements with `arithmatex`.  This makes escape handling a bit better from Markdown to HTML `\$` will ignore dollar in markdown and then become just `$` in HTML.  But it shouldn't get processed by `tex2jax` because arithmatex didn't wrap it in a span with an `arithmatex` class.  It is still recommended to not use `tex2jax`, but this is how you can use it without issue (based on my limited testing).

```
    ignoreClass: ".*|",
    processClass: "arithmatex"
```

wsgy.py

import os

in my flask app:

import os
...
from prometheus_client import multiprocess
from prometheus_client import CollectorRegistry
from prometheus_flask_exporter import PrometheusMetrics
from prometheus_flask_exporter import DEFAULT_REGISTRY
...
app = Flask(__name__)

registry = DEFAULT_REGISTRY
if os.environ.get('prometheus_multiproc_dir'):
    stats_dir = os.environ.get('prometheus_multiproc_dir')
    if not os.path.exists(stats_dir):
        os.makedirs(stats_dir)
    registry = CollectorRegistry()
    multiprocess.MultiProcessCollector(registry)

```

I was thinking about using a getattr, but a generator function/lamba is a much better idea.

Each session is bound to a single user via a unique ID.  If two people are chatting on your site (i.e. PersonA and PersonB), their two sessions are isolated from one another by design.  PersonB should never be able to affect PersonA's session and vice-versa, your backend would seriously breaking the "generally accepted design" of server-side-sessions if you were tracking and adjusting one user's session based on the activity of another.

Your "chat" information should not be stored in the session and should be in some other "chat" datastore.

Just made a few tests with different ResNet models and I also experienced crashes. 

Issue appears tied with a memory that isn't released during training. No problem with ResNet34 or 50, but it got problematic with 101. Have you looked at the GPU usage immediatly after launching the training (nvidia-smi) to confirm you have same issue? 

I also noticed apparent memory leak when running large embeddings. A quick turnaround is to add a gc() within the training loop after each couple of batch (not necessary to add a gc() within the eval data loop). You can do it either in `mx.model.FeedForward.create` or `mx.model.buckets` (I only used the later but should work for the usual training function). Good news is that it doesn't slow down noticeably the training and finetune ResNet101 wasn't crashing anymore and GPU memory remained below 4Go on 8 samples. 

@thirdwing Any idea whether this memory issue could better be handled than with gc()? If performance isn't affected, I wonder if a quick PR with the gc() would be worth.

@s-cotter,

> Is there are reason to implement SFTP as a operator rather than hook like the existing ftp hook ? 

Yes, in this addition that uses paramiko library for ssh, SFTP is seen as one of the operator, that works on top of SSH Transport.  Also by the philosophy of airflow, the way i understood, I would use hooks to manage the underlying connections and operators to actually perform any functions using that hook. Although I am seeing mixups in hooks & operator at some places in current code.

> It would be nice to just be able to switch hooks (ftp->sftp) and have the same interface/functionality. In my case I need other features needed by the ftp hook for SFTP.

Sure, that would be good flexibility to have, but Paramiko is all about secure connections using ssh trasport, and dont support ftp or ftp over SSL/TLS. Although since it provides most of standard ftp functions in its sftp implementation ( ex: getCWD() ), we can create wrapper functions in SFTPOperator, if the compatibility and easy switching between ftp and sftp is needed. That would be a separate change though and out of the scope of this PR.

How would you want to cache them? At the moment, it only fetches a bug's summary from bugzilla once per page load, so moving the mouse over a bug link, away from it, and back to it will only make one request to bugzilla (unless you do it really quickly before the first network request can finish). If more than one commit references the same bug number, it WILL make a network request for each one.

Should I just make an array on the user's browser or something to store each bug's summary and try searching in it before making a network request? That would still be blown away if the user refreshes the page, but I guess that's good since the summary/status could have changed.

Or what about storing that array more persistently in localStorage or indexedDB or something along with a timestamp so we don't hit the network unless it's either not stored at all or not checked since a certain time?

Not quite. It's more like a class that identifies new failures according to specific criteria, so that they can be added to the database. It used to actually do the IO, but I factored that out :) There's only one concrete implementation at the moment, but one can certainly imagine more (e.g. one for previously unseen crashes, one for log entries, etc.)

In general one would hope that a Matcher object will be able to match new failures against the ones identified here, but there isn't a 1:1 correspondence. For example the current test failure matcher might match against intermittents identified by the test failure creator, but so would an alternate matcher that did an imprecise match on the message field.

So there is no guarantee that the ``Message`` key will be included in the returned response. The response is strictly generated off of the HTTP response from the server so if ``Message`` was not included in the response from the server, boto3 will not include it in the response. You should be able to check this by adding ``boto3.set_stream_logger('botocore')`` to line before the client call.

As to the messaging you are getting in the console, it may not be the actual value that matches up to the ``Message`` key. The console may have some logic to construct that message from other values in the response.

Hey @matthieugomez, thanks for the question. Yes that's intended and it turns out that both are called. 

Let me explain. First we get all the [matching substring blocks](https://github.com/seatgeek/fuzzywuzzy/blob/51f560f19e1f6dff76cad1df4a2549a542002544/fuzzywuzzy/fuzz.py#L74-L75):

```python
m = SequenceMatcher(None, shorter, longer)
blocks = m.get_matching_blocks()
```

This results in two tuples describing matching blocks:
```python
blocks = [
  Match(i=0, j=30, n=8),
  Match(i=18, j=38, n=0)  # the last triple is a dummy, `(len(a), len(b), 0)`, and is the only triple with `n==0`
]
```

For strings `a` and `b`, a `Match` as explained in a difflib comment [here](https://github.com/enthought/Python-2.7.3/blob/69fe0ffd2d85b4002cacae1f28ef2eb0f25e16ae/Lib/difflib.py#L463-L464) means that:
```python
a[i:i+n] == b[j:j+n]
```


Thus, for strings `mariners vs angels` and `los angeles angels at seattle mariners` we have one Match (and one dummy block for the full string, see [here](https://github.com/enthought/Python-2.7.3/blob/69fe0ffd2d85b4002cacae1f28ef2eb0f25e16ae/Lib/difflib.py#L471-L472)):
```python
"mariners vs angels"[0:0+8] == "los angeles angels at seattle mariners"[30:30+8] == "mariners"
```

We then run over each of the matching blocks and the dummy block representing the full string in [this way](https://github.com/seatgeek/fuzzywuzzy/blob/51f560f19e1f6dff76cad1df4a2549a542002544/fuzzywuzzy/fuzz.py#L83-L94):
```python
    scores = []
    for block in blocks:
        long_start = block[1] - block[0] if (block[1] - block[0]) > 0 else 0
        long_end = long_start + len(shorter)
        long_substr = longer[long_start:long_end]

        m2 = SequenceMatcher(None, shorter, long_substr)
        r = m2.ratio()
        if r > .995:
            return 100
        else:
            scores.append(r)
```
If you step through this block you'll see that we'll run both ratios:

```python
# This is a ratio on the matching block substring
SequenceMatcher(None, "mariners vs angels", "mariners").ratio()

# This is a ratio on the length - m substring
SequenceMatcher(None, "mariners vs angels", "t seattle mariners").ratio()
```

This gives us two scores `0.615` and `0.444`. We return the higher of the two--which is why it looks like we called `ratio` on just `mariners`.

@mmerickel asked me to chip in via email:

> The core of the issue is that there is currently a lot of undefined behavior around transaction boundaries when using pyramid_tm. The transactions could be lazily created by tweens prior to the pyramid_tm ingress, and people could easily be using the transactional data after the egress of pyramid_tm (in other tweens such as exception views, or even in response callbacks). Once the transaction is closed the next time someone touches a var a new transaction is lazily opened with no one to close it leaving dangling connections/resources.

> The list of features I'm interested in are:

> - Verify if ingress works properly with an already-active transaction.

Any existing transaction would be aborted when pyramid_tm calls ``manager.begin()``
 
> - Expand the transaction boundaries to encompass exception views and possibly response callbacks.

What to do for exceptions at transaction commit that are un-retryable or out-of-attempts? You may still need exception views for this.
 
> - Raise exception if a transaction is already active at ingress.

There is no public interface in transaction for this, but the check would be: ``assert manager._txn is None``. Another alternative would be to check that the transaction has no data managers: ``assert not txn._resources``. This really should use a public method.
 
> - Raise exception if a transaction is created after egress.

Not sure how to enforce this without changing transaction. You'd need a way to make TransactionManager.get() raise an error instead of implicitly creating a new transaction. I view the current behaviour of ``get()`` as a bit of a wart as unlike ``begin()``, it does not call ``newTransaction(txn)`` on any synchronizers. Fixing this would also fix the problem of unexpected new transactions at ingress.

--

The other pain point currently is that the request object is reused between attempts leading to ``DetachedInstanceErrors``. Most of the above problems are a direct result of using a tween rather than WSGI middleware. The only downside I can see to using *repoze.tm2* would be that you would need a way to register exception views for un-retryable / attempted-out exceptions during commit. Maybe we need to make that work instead. Or maybe subclass Router.

You can find the generated wheels at https://ci.appveyor.com/project/matham/nixpy/build/1.0.47/artifacts. To use it, just download it and then download numpy and h5py wheels from www.lfd.uci.edu/~gohlke/pythonlibs/ and install them with pip install wheel_names. Then it all works (tested with `import nixio`). No need for any exe, including no need to download and isntall nix.exe.

I'll make a pr with the appveyor file and code changes to support this if you're interested. Then you could upload these wheels to pypi and someone could just do `pip install nixio` and it'll work.

If you want the logging to go to a separate file for each component, file the instructions in our docs [here](http://streamparse.readthedocs.io/en/stable/quickstart.html?highlight=logging#logging).  

If you want to log things to the Storm worker logs (only/strongly recommended if you're on Storm 1.0+), you just need to make sure your `config.json` does not set specify a log path. That will let you use the very nice Storm UI log search interface for your logs.

One tip is to try to always use `self.logger` for logging inside your bolt/spout.  

If you want logs for other modules to show up in your Storm logs, you must set their logging levels appropriately (e.g., `logging.getLogger('pykafka.broker').setLevel(logging.WARNING)`).  The root logger will be attached to the appropriate handler to send your logs either to separate files or the storm logs, but unless you set the logging level for your loggers, the messages won't go anyway.  The logging level in `config.json` just sets the level for your bolts and spouts' loggers, and the root logger's *handler* (but not the root logger itself).  This may change in the future as noted in #290.

@dalan-stripe That's not the way to update an account. You should not be overriding the entire `legal_entity` hash. Doing this will automatically set new params for what's in your hash and _unset_ existing params/properties in the hash. Instead, you should set each property separately:

```
acc.legal_entity.dob.day = 1
acc.legal_entity.dob.month = 1
acc.legal_entity.dob.year = 1980
acc.legal_entity.first_name = "John"
acc.legal_entity.last_name = "Smith"
acc.legal_entity.type = "individual"

acc.save()
```

Contrary to what I said before, probably fine to just adapt the current region. I don't think we need to adapt the CLAModel as part of the initial change. We can make the region available and test it out before we spend the effort adapting the CLAModel.

Really, we don't need to initialize these numpy arrays. We could just set `self._overlaps` and `self._boostedOverlaps` to `None`. We build a new array every timestep, so these arrays are never actually used. If we allocate an array in the `__init__` method, it implies that we're going to reuse it.

This would save us from having `numpy.zeros` calls all over the place. Whether we reuse the arrays or not, there only needs to be one code location that calls `numpy.zeros` (or rather, one for each of the two arrays).
This one can't really be done until all other encoders are integrated.

On the subject of MANIFEST.in and superfluous package_data, according to https://docs.python.org/3.4/distutils/sourcedist.html#specifying-the-files-to-distribute:

> If you do supply your own MANIFEST, you must specify everything: the default set of files described above does not apply in this case.

I interpret that to mean that if you use MANIFEST.in (which we do), then it doesn't matter what is in `package_data`.  The files need to be added in MANIFEST.in.  In which case, we can remove `package_data` from setup.py.

I think the "right" thing to do is to change [`outputs`](https://github.com/mrcslws/nupic.core/blob/314ab7cb0c01d47d08114cefdba7feaa9346c605/src/nupic/regions/PyRegion.cpp#L942) from `py::Dict` to a different Python object that [emulates a dict](https://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html?emulating-container-types#emulating-container-types) by defining `__getitem__` but throwing an exception when `__setitem__` is called.

This change would be isolated to the single function and will enforce that callers properly populate the array rather than trying to replace it (incurring an unnecessary array allocation).

The big question is how to construct the dict-like class in C++ code. A namedtuple would work but doesn't have a C++ type since it is implemented with a hacky Python eval. It would also be somewhat easy to do in Python by subclassing `tuple` and defining `__getitem__`. But I'm not sure how exactly to do these things in C++.

Cons:

I would use 400 (bad request) or alternatively 415 (unsupported media type) instead.

5xx errors are server errors, where as 4xx errors are client errors. In this case, it's a client side error since ``Content-Type`` is not set in the request, and thus the request body is assumed to be ``application/json``. The client should have set ``Content-Type`` to ``application/xml`` in which case the request body would be parsed correctly.

415 would be best suited if we require ``Content-Type`` to be set and thus don't have a default value (but this might break existing integrations). So probably a 400 with a proper error message is the best.

Just my _personal_ opinion (not join2, not my employer or whoever).

IMHO the reasoning against GPL is a logical circle. So far the reasoning is

- We needed GPL _back then_ because companies feared Open Source.
- Nowadays, companies live in Open Source and release everything in Open Source anyway. There was a _change in culture_ and a _rush to release_. (Only slightly exaggerating the points made, just for clarification.)
- Because companies live in Open Source, one needs a "more permissive" license and not something viral like GPL.
- That is required, _because_ companies need the possibility to _hide back_ something to rise their share holders value.

This is contradictory.

If a company really lives in Open Source (A)GPL would _not_ hinder them in anything. They'd release all their stuff anyway, and if we see a _change in culture_ (A)GPL would be a natural thing to them. It would also ensure that the competitor does the same. If we do not see the change in culture we should head for it, again we are at (A)GPL.

Companies only need something "more permissive" if they do _not_ want to share everything. To keep something private to be "better", to "raise their corporate value". (This is also in line put forward by @lnielsen in Dev Forum.) I buy this point as a reason, but I do not support it.

At some point it IMHO boils down to the question what I buy from a company.  In Open Source I would not buy _software_, but I would probably buy the hosting and other services around it. So it is _unimportant_ that the software is really open or not. I'd even go so far as to say that _the software_ as such is entirely unimportant and interchangeable in this framework as long as the service stays the same. Hypothetically, Open Source would give me as a customer the advantage of the vague possibility to switch my service provider, so I could argue that this is an advantage for a business running on Open Source software.

Furthermore, where I usually lack openness is when it comes down to interfaces. Naturally, the interface definition has to live within the code where I need this interface. _At least_ this definition has to be free to ensure that I could live on an interface even if a company just _looses interest_ in supporting anything on it. (Here I could follow @david-caro argument for LGPL, or more generally non-AGPL, as a minimal requirement.) Much of the hackish stuff I need to do (I'd not call this "development") is exactly fixing such issue, working around it, up to the point of reverse engineering some strange protocol or format.

Given software being interchangeable, but interfaces are not, while I also run more and more on services that communicate with one another via interfaces, IMHO today GPL is required even more than ever to ensure that free software _stays_ free and _usable_.

To me, as a tax payer, the latter is also of foremost importance. Just if one wants to make a monetary point.

The more natural reasoning is that you are of course free to swing your fists.  But this _freedom ends_ at the tip of my nose. So what you call restriction of freedom may just be ensuring its persistence.

I agree, this is definitely a bug. `SMTPMailer.send()` unconditionally calls `connection.quit()` which itself unconditionally calls `connection.close()`, so the SMTPMailer is useless after one message has been sent. Given that SMTPMailers are registered as utilities, that seems wrong. And given that the `zope-sendmail` command (from `queue.py`) will try to send messages in a loop using the same SMTPMailer object, that *also* seems wrong.

@GVRV I did something like this below (@baratrion's idea):
```python
def add_sentry_handler_to_task_loggers(client, loglevel=logging.ERROR):
    """
    Similar to what raven's `register_logger_signal` does, but registers
    the celery `after_setup_task_logger` to add the SentryHandler to all
    celery task loggers (register_logger_signal only registers the signal
    to configure global loggers)
    """
    filter_ = CeleryFilter()

    handler = SentryHandler(client)
    handler.setLevel(loglevel)
    handler.addFilter(filter_)
    handler.setFormatter(TaskFormatter())

    def process_logger_event(
        sender, logger, loglevel, logfile, format, colorize, **kwargs
    ):
        logger.addHandler(handler)

    # See http://docs.celeryproject.org/en/latest/userguide/signals.html#after-setup-task-logger
    after_setup_task_logger.connect(process_logger_event, weak=False)
```

I checked the rdflib parse tree, and I think I know now where the problem lies:
```
SelectQuery(
    p = Project(
        p = Filter(
            p = BGP(
                triples = [(rdflib.term.Variable(u'a'), rdflib.term.URIRef(u'http://www.example.de#rel'), rdflib.term.Variable(u'b'))]
                _vars = set([rdflib.term.Variable(u'b'), rdflib.term.Variable(u'a')])
                )
            expr = Builtin_NOTEXISTS(
                graph = GroupGraphPatternSub(
                    part = [TriplesBlock_{'_vars': set([rdflib.term.Variable(u'b'), rdflib.term.Variable(u'a')]), 'triples': [[rdflib.term.Variable(u'a'), rdflib.term.URIRef(u'http://www.example.de#rel'), rdflib.term.Variable(u'b')]]}]
                    _vars = set([rdflib.term.Variable(u'b'), rdflib.term.Variable(u'a')])
                    )
                _vars = set([rdflib.term.Variable(u'b'), rdflib.term.Variable(u'a')])
                )
            _vars = set([rdflib.term.Variable(u'b'), rdflib.term.Variable(u'a')])
            )
        PV = [rdflib.term.Variable(u'a'), rdflib.term.Variable(u'b')]
        _vars = set([rdflib.term.Variable(u'b'), rdflib.term.Variable(u'a')])
        )
    datasetClause = None
    PV = [rdflib.term.Variable(u'a'), rdflib.term.Variable(u'b')]
    _vars = set([rdflib.term.Variable(u'b'), rdflib.term.Variable(u'a')])
    )
```
As you can see, the built in NOTEXISTS expression node does not parse the inner FILTER statement at all. It just disappears from the tree. Presumably it does not expect an inner filter statement?
