@jberkus 
It is not hard to implement such hook. If we get non zero exit code from the script, we will just dynamically set `nofailover` tag to `True`.

But I don't really like to have such functionality, because it will become the biggest foot-shotgun ever...

It seems that we are putting together a lot of different things here:
* writability and how to check it?
  * select pg_is_in_recovery()?
  * CREATE TABLE/INSERT per tablespace? 
* critical conditions
  * disk (or quota) full on one or more filesystems
  * faulty hardware
  * I/O errors in the syslog

Right now on every iteration of HA loop Patroni is verifying that `pg_is_in_recovery()` returns `False`, what generally means that writes are possible. I absolutely agree that this might be not enough. For example one of the filesystems can become full, read-only or not accessible due to different reasons.

Most of the checks for the above mentioned conditions must be executed not only on the master but on all nodes in the cluster, otherwise you can really get a cascade of failovers. How are you going to run DDL/DML on replicas? Even ability to perform INSERT doesn't really gives us a guaranty that data would be persisted on disk (problems with fsync).

In wast majority of cases (except disk space issues) "failing" node must be removed from cluster and replaced with the new one. This action should be done either by some person or external tooling rather than Patroni.

And regarding diskspace issues: there could be dozens of different reasons why disk space is eaten and in my opinion none of them deserves "automated" switchover, because at the end you'll get into the same situation on the replica and have another switchover...

I’d expect everyone deploying Patroni to be doing  scheduled restarts differently: by employing some cron jobs or running scripts to trigger the equivalent cloud provider capabilities. This would lead to a lot of extra maintenance when running at scale. We can avoid that by providing a simple in-core implementation that would cover a majority of possible use cases.

I'm not concerned about execution time; I'm concerned about unmaintainability and unreadability of the code.  As far as I can tell, we repeatedly parse connection URLs into a dictionary, then reassemble them as a space-delimited connection string, which is all completely unnecessary.

Since I'm going to overhaul how we manage connection information anyway, it seems like we should just stick to storing everything as a url, and using it as a url.

Here is your problem:
```yaml
restapi:
  listen: 0.0.0.0:8008
  connect_address: 127.0.0.1:8008

restapi:
  listen: 127.0.0.1:8008
  connect_address: 127.0.0.1:8008
```

Restapi configuration is wrong. Patroni uses REST API for communication between nodes.
When it will try to access 127.0.0.1:8008 - it will connect to itself instead of another node.

Just change it to:
```yaml
restapi: # db01
  listen: 172.27.167.61:5432
  connect_address: 172.27.167.61:5432

restapi: # db02
  listen: 172.27.167.62:5432
  connect_address: 172.27.167.62:5432
```

and it will work.

First bug : 

From what I understand you want `x_in` to be batched right? You can read more about minibatching in the docs but basically what you want is : 

    x_vals=np.zeros((64,64))
    x_in = dy.inputTensor(x_vals,batched = True) # The last dimension of x_vals 
                                                 # will be used as a batch dimension 
    w = model.parameters_from_numpy(xavier_normal_init(shape))
    b = model.parameters_from_numpy(constant_init((shape[1], )))
    w = dy.parameter(w)
    b = dy.parameter(b)
    f = nonlinearity_from_string(nonlinearity)
    return f(x_in * w + b)

Second bug : 

The second argument of `dy.pickneglogsoftmax` should just be a (python) integer so 

    y = 0
    cost = dy.sum_batches(dy.pickneglogsoftmax(h, y))

should work.

There is no specific operation to average over a batch but you can just sum and then divide by the batch size.

This is not so simple, as manylinux wheels are currently built on the [pypa docker](https://github.com/pypa/manylinux), which is CentOS5 and doesn't support CUDA (note that in our [Travis CI configuration](https://github.com/clab/dynet/blob/master/.travis/install_dependencies.sh), CUDA is installed "natively" on the Linux machine with manual install and not pip). In fact, TF, which [does provide](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/tensorflow-gpu) GPU-supported wheels, builds them on a newer toolset and so they are not really `manylinux1`-compatible (tensorflow/tensorflow#5033 and tensorflow/tensorflow#8802). It's possible to do that for DyNet too but without a pre-made Docker image it's complicated.
Maybe when `manylinux2` exists, it will be easier.

I think the right way to implement this is to do two custom ops, one for
the x<0 case and the other for the x>=0 case, then you the select operation
together with those two. I looked into implementing select and the two
operators together in one op and using the packet operations myself, but
the way Eigen is designed makes this difficult to do (basically, you need
to have access to the vector size of the packet type, which could have been
a template parameter but isn't).

I assume this will be faster than the non-packet version (if if you're
doing both the -x and x cases every time), but this would need to be
empirically looked at.

> [Can you] toss in short examplar code snippets illustrating the problem?

> ["Always use a client"] interferes with the "stackable" pattern we use to allow batches / transactions to proxy for their underlying connections.

At the moment, one can use a batch or a transaction as a context manager an *not* pass it everywhere, or call methods through it.  E.g.:

```python
    from gcloud import datastore
    with datastore.Transaction():
        keys = [entity.key for entity in some_list]
        datastore.delete(keys)
```

This works because the `_require_connection` code is willing to look up the current batch / transaction from a thread-local stack, falling back to use the global default.  Calling API functions through the notional `Client` instance will defeat that stacking.  Note that a object methods will be also lose that stacking.

> In every place we just visited in the #825 PRs, we would probably end up replacing the connection=None argument and the _require_connection() inside the method with client=None and _require_client(), all FBO developers who a) don't want to use the default project ID, and b) don't want to pass it to the Bucket / Topic constructors or the list_buckets / list_topics / list_subscriptions queries.

Per #825, *all* of the methods / functions that trigger remote API calls now take an optional `connection` argument (modulo #858 which isn't yet merged).  They all fall back to using either the "stack" (for batch/transaction support) or the global default.  If we implement "always use a client", then we would have to undo all that work (which cleaned up the code a *lot*, and go back to using `self.connection` attributes / properties.

> This line of development could logically be pushed to a point where we move all the API methods onto the Client, dropping all the intermediate classes in favor of pure imperative code.

It is a bit of a strawman, but having "always use a client" as the access pattern reduces the desirability of the various client-side objects:  they become basically thin wrappers over the client.

This isn't super easy, and requires that the OS of the instance be able to parse user data and write out files.

For an LoadBalancer object that you named 'ELBNAME' you would then put a Ref("ELBNAME") in the UserData for the instance in whatever format you need it to be in for the OS to read from the user data and write out the name for you.

If you go here, and then take a look at the first example of Template Examples  here: http://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSCloudFormation/latest/UserGuide/aws-properties-as-launchconfig.html#cfn-as-launchconfig-userdata

You should see something similar - where they are passing in the WebServerPort to the userdata of a launch configuration for autoscaling.

Worth understanding userdata - it makes life a lot easier in general:

http://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSEC2/latest/UserGuide/user-data.html

I've also been stuck on trying to use `google.cloud` for one of my apps that's managed through Pants/Pex. But I figured out, but I don't remember how, that if I copied the `.dist-info` folders for all packages that contained a `namespace_packages.txt` file into the root folder of the `.pex` then it would work. 

So I have a little shell script that unzips the pex, finds all the packages with a namespace_packages.txt, copies, rezips and then take it from there.

In case that can help anyone else out. :)

Thanks for the preview of the solution, @scanny . Cannot tell you how useful your comments are in implementing the few outstanding to-do features in what truly is a fantastic library.

For anyone else looking for a full working solution, here is what I came up with, to generate the single line that inserts the TOC field. Auto-updating the TOC was outside of my capabilities for the time being so I'll leave it to someone else to take over:

		paragraph = self.document.add_paragraph()
		run = paragraph.add_run()
		fldChar = OxmlElement('w:fldChar')  # creates a new element
		fldChar.set(qn('w:fldCharType'), 'begin')  # sets attribute on element
		instrText = OxmlElement('w:instrText')
		instrText.set(qn('xml:space'), 'preserve')  # sets attribute on element
		instrText.text = 'TOC \o "1-3" \h \z \u'  # change 1-3 depending on heading levels you need
				
		fldChar2 = OxmlElement('w:fldChar')  # creates a new element
		fldChar2.set(qn('w:fldCharType'), 'separate')  # sets attribute on element
		fldChar3 = OxmlElement('w:t')  # creates a new element
		fldChar3.text = "No table of contents entries found."
		fldChar2.append(fldChar3)

		fldChar4 = OxmlElement('w:fldChar')  # creates a new element
		fldChar4.set(qn('w:fldCharType'), 'end')  # sets attribute on element

		r_element = run._r
		r_element.append(fldChar)  # adds new element as last child
		r_element.append(instrText)
		r_element.append(fldChar2)  # adds new element as last child
		r_element.append(fldChar4)
		p_element = paragraph._p

Just declaring the type to be unicode without specifying the encoding 
that is being used will be very fragile.  It will work for you, 
apparently, with files created in one specific way, in one language, but 
files created using different software may well use a different encoding 
and will break your code.

If you don't have any experience working with unicode, as your question 
and solution imply, you might start by taking a look at 
https://docs.python.org/3/howto/unicode.html .

@relevitt all those are valid concerns, which is why the transition to Python 3 has taken as long as it has (also because the Python powers-at-be jumped the gun a little) and believe me, I have no desire to try and force people into something that they don't want to.

**BUT!**. This **will** happen at some point anyway. Committing to Python 2 compatibility forever is quite simply too restrictive. Maintaining the 2-3 compatible code makes the task if maintenance and development more difficult, a lot less fun and we cut of selves of from any Python 3-only features.

So. Each of the issues will need to be dealt with at some point anyway. Why not start in good time before everything shuts down.

 * For your code base, to start to port it and see how difficult it is.
 * For twisted, to see whether you use any of the problematic parts
 * For xmms2, to see whether you will need to port that yourself also or move to another library*

I know this part can seem harsh, but really, it isn't fair to make us responsible for other peoples unmaintained code either.

@DXCanas Nope this use case is not possible, since the device owner/teacher have their own server laptop and the kids access through tablets specifically.

I think the original double-click issue might be hyper specific to the card layout, since it may be the only page where a card will be replaced with another card.

So the proportionate solution, I think, would be to delay rendering those replacement cards. This could even be done in a super-local way in the Carousel or Card-Grid components.
Refs: #3228

> This means that it is impossible to load any frontend assets on another template without also loading them into the base template.

Not sure that's so -> creating a new asset means creating a new hook that inherits from `FrontEndAssetHook` or `WebpackBundleHook`. Calling `{% webpack_assets 'slug' %}` renders it.

The proposed fix for this is to emulate Studio behaviour and write license data directly onto ContentNode models.

This will involve:

Deleting the License model.

Adding license_name and license_description fields to the ContentNode model. For now, this will involve complete denormalization of the license data onto the ContentNode.

In future, when we have frontend support for known licenses (like CC-BY), the license_name will be an identifier, rather than a title, and the frontend will handle display of the license name and description for known license types.

In the case of unknown license types the frontend will fall back to using the license_name and license_description from the database.

Note - this approach also allows for proper internationalization of known licenses in the frontend.

The config space is the domain of the operator, not the charm author. We
shouldn't bleed implementation details into the names of the options they
are setting. If the base layer is providing options that are too incorrect,
you can override them, if you don't know what it is providing then create
the layers.yaml file with your includes and run 'charm build' and look at
the output.

If this is a frequent issue (unintentional overrides of a previous layer
config) we can issue warnings during merge, but for the most part I'd
assume this was an intentional override.

Your assumption is that sending the detach sequence to the socket will close the socket (which you test with `assert_socket_closed_with_keys`), but that doesn't actually happen. As far as I can tell, all it does is tell the daemon "stop sending data through that socket". The client side is still responsible for closing the connection.

However, I was able to trigger a `socket.error` (Broken pipe) using this code snippet:

```python
import docker

c = docker.APIClient()
ctnr = c.create_container('busybox', 'cat', detach=True, stdin_open=True, tty=True)
c.start(ctnr)
sock = c.attach_socket({'stdin': True, 'stdout': True, 'stream': True, 'detachKeys': 'ctrl-x'})
sock.send('abc')
print(sock.recv(3))  # should read 'abc' back
s.send(chr(24))  # ctrl-x
s.send('abc')  # error: [Errno 32] Broken pipe
```

On Mac OS X (and others), we get a BSD-derived `echo` which only understands the `-n` flag (don't print trailing newline char). So adding `-e` will cause the installer to literally print `-e` on those platforms. And the ANSI coloring works just fine on Ubuntu without the `-e` flag (but not on CoreOS, where it shows up incorrectly as you described).

The code in question is [here](https://github.com/deis/deis/blob/master/deisctl/Makefile#L16-L29) and [here](https://github.com/deis/deis/blob/master/client/Makefile#L19-L28). I don't see a simple way to fix this in general other than removing the ANSI color codes.

Oh, I know why this was originally added: the originating issue is [this one](https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/110). The idea is that conda should not update Python 2 to Python 3 unless specifically requested. It looks like fix actually goes further: it will not update, say, Python 2.6 to Python 2.7 without specifically requesting it.

This is of course a very reasonable thing to do, and given the limitations of the current solver architecture that function is necessary. With my larger solver fix, I'm not convinced it will be necessary any longer. But at the very least, it will be possible to tell conda to _leave Python alone_ unless it can't avoid it :-)

Thanks for the PR.  Trying to determine the default Lua version by using a subprocess seems slow and brittle, and is probably not something we want to be doing in conda.  In particular because only very few people use Lua, but all conda users are going to be affected by this change.  As far as I'm concerned, conda (unlike conda-build) should not have any features hard-coded to support special languages.  My point of view is that conda is a language (which includes Python) agnostic package manager.  Conda-build, on the other hand, may (and does) contain special code to support all sorts of commonly used scenarios.

```bash
if [ "${CONDA_QUIET}" = "0" ]; then
  echo "INFO: $(_get_sourced_filename) made the following environmental changes:"
  diff -U 0 -rN /tmp/old-env-$$.txt /tmp/new-env-$$.txt | tail -n +4 | grep "^-.*\|^+.*" | grep -v "CONDA_BACKUP_" | sort
fi
```

There have been several PRs, but none have implemented this in a way that we think is wise.  Activation is more than just modifying PATH - it's also running other scripts in the etc/conda/activate.d folder.  Any accepted solution needs to do everything that the current activate.bat script does:

* get path changes from conda itself (as opposed to hard-coding changes in powershell somehow)
* call all additional .ps1 scripts in activate.d folders

The latter point there is tricky.  Since powershell support is so new, it would be nice if the powershell scripts also supported .bat files in the absence of any similarly-named .ps1 file.  Some packages, such as GDAL, depend on these scripts to set environment variables.

@kalefranz has done a ton of work to rework how activation scripts work in the 4.4.x branch.  It may be worth postponing any further effort on powershell until 4.4.x gets out of pre-release and into common usage.  It's much faster and simpler with regards to this activation stuff.

import re

def get_iscn(variant_obj):
    """Returns ISCN coordinates

    Args:
        variant_obj(dict)

    Returns:
        iscn_coordinates(str)
    """

    start=variant_obj["position"]
    end=variant_obj["end"]
    chromosome=variant_obj["chromosome"]
    variant_type=variant_obj["sub_category"]
    posB = None

    if ":" in variant_obj["alt"] and not ">" in variant_obj["alt"]:
        B=re.split("[],[]",variant_obj["alt"]);
        for string in B:
            if string.count(":"):
                lst=string.split(":");
                chromosomeB=lst[0]
                posB=lst[1]

    iscn_coordinates=""
    #del, dup, and inv are printed in a similar fashion
    if variant_type == "del" or variant_type == "inv" or variant_type == "dup":
        
        iscn_coordinates="{}:g.{}_{}{}".format(chromosome,start,end,variant_type)

    #print interchromosomal translocation
    elif not chromosome == chromosomeB and posB:
        ter_A=[]
        ter_B=[]

        iscn_coordinates += "g.[{}:{}_{}::{}:{}_{}]\n".format(chromosome,ter_A[0],start,chromosomeB,posB,ter_B[0])
        iscn_coordinates += "g.[{}:{}_{}::{}:{}_{}]".format(chromosomeB,posB,ter_B[1],chromosome,ter_A[1],start)
    
    return(iscn_coordinates)

The callback passed to `run_transaction` should not try to `commit` the transaction; leave that to the wrapper. 

We need `savepoint_state.cockroach_restart` because the semantics of the `cockroach_restart` psuedo-savepoint are not quite the same as regular savepoints (releasing the `cockroach_restart` savepoint commits the transaction). We don't support savepoints or nested transactions in general, only the specific form used by `run_transaction`. If we hard-coded the name then applications could try to use nested transactions in ways that would fail mysteriously (and while it would change the error message you see here, it wouldn't make it correct to call `commit` inside the `run_transaction` callback).

Before I looked at the link you provided, I saw this in the log data you pasted:

```
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/elasticsearch/connection/http_urllib3.py", line 99, in perform_request
    raise ConnectionTimeout('TIMEOUT', str(e), e)
elasticsearch.exceptions.ConnectionTimeout: ConnectionTimeout caused by - ReadTimeoutError(HTTPConnectionPool(host=u'localhost', port=9200): Read timed out. (read timeout=120))
```
A timeout, such as this one, means that Curator is not able to get the data or response it expects from Elasticsearch in the given time.  In this case, it's 120 seconds.  This differs from what you have in that wiki link, which says 600 seconds.

Having perused the contents of that wiki entry, I see that you're using Azure for your snapshot repository.  Remote repositories can take a lot longer to respond,  I suggest at least a 600 second timeout, if not 900.  The reality is that Curator isn't a special client tool.  It just does API calls to Elasticsearch and reports the results.  If Curator timed out at 120 seconds, chances are very good that a curl call of the same would suffer similarly.

Some questions to help troubleshoot this further would be to ascertain how many snapshots are in these repositories, and other related questions, like, "how often are they captured?"  One reason for timeout issues could be the large volume of segments having to be tracked and accounted for.  You're deleting snapshots after 7 days, but are you only capturing one every day, or more frequently?  How many indices are there in a given snapshot?

This should be addressed by #595 and #596 with Curator 4.0 imminent.

If you feel this is in error, please feel free to reopen this ticket or open another.

The portions you are showing are only revealing methods within the elasticsearch-py and urllib3 modules.  Note the 2nd column, which contains the name of the module, while the 3rd column shows the method being called, and on what line number.  Only the very bottom method is a `curator.api.snapshot` call.  

Look at what's happening.  The first `_make_request` line shows a 504 error, which is a gateway timeout, [which is](http://www.checkupdown.com/status/E504.html)

> A server (not necessarily a Web server) is acting as a gateway or proxy to fulfil the request by the client  to access the requested URL. This server did not receive a timely response from an upstream server it accessed to deal with your HTTP request.
>
>This usually means that the upstream server is down (no response to the gateway/proxy)

But that 504 is probably indicating the same disconnect you saw with your curl call, in that `wait_for_completion` did not seem to wait for the complete time. It seems that the urllib3 code that the elasticsearch library is using is just as confused by the odd timeout.  In both cases, the snapshot has been started, and is still going, but `wait_for_timeout` is not waiting.

And because of the 504, it thinks it failed, hence the retries and the failure messages, but these are not from Curator, but from the urllib3 calls in the elasticsearch-py module. It never goes back to Curator until it has retried 3 times and failed with each. Each failed retry within the elasticsearch module results in a call to the `log_request_fail` method.  This confirms what I have repeatedly stated: Curator itself is not retrying, but rather something farther up the chain.  

My suspicions at this point are two:

1. There is some transport layer (gateway or proxy) between you and S3 that is perhaps causing some of these issues.  For example, an aggressive firewall rule that disallows HTTP keepalive connections, or those longer than 60 seconds, might do something like this.
2. Since you have an older version of Elasticsearch (1.3.2)—and correspondingly, the S3 plugin, there's a bug in your version of the Elasticsearch code that is signaling an improper failure, so the elasticsearch python library is instigating a retry, but that retry fails.  I did not see your exact issue in the Changelogs from Elasticsearch 1.3.3 up to 1.3.9, but there were many snapshot related fixes merged in those versions.  Updating may solve the problem, and certainly wouldn't hurt if it didn't.

Hi @nneul, the best way I can think to accomplish your scenario would be to use generic update combined with the @file syntax to define your rules in a JSON snippet and then load them.  The command would look something like:

`az network nsg update -g yourRG -n yourNSG --set securityRules=@rules.json`

Then your JSON would have to be the JSON array definition of the rules you want to create. In this way, you can add all your rules with a single PUT call. Does that work for your scenario?

Hi @madhugilla 

I wrote a wiki on how we do our testing (including tests locations):
https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/wiki/Contributing-to-the-tests

At a glance, we hook requests using vcrpy to record HTTP calls into files. When we replay the tests (in "playback" mode), the framework automatically open the files and answer what was recorded. This way, we do not need any mock, we use the regular Python code. We coded a wrapper around vcrpy, but it's just a tools for convenience and it's not mandatory (the Python CLI team uses vcrpy too with no wrapper).

Please read the wiki and tell me how (or not) this is answering to your question. This way I can help you more precisely and/or update my document if something is unclear.

So the current `legacy` will work if you do something like:

```bash
git lfs install  # or whatever there install command is, I forget?
pre-commit install --hook-type pre-push  # no `--force`
```

This will
1. `git-lfs` will write `.git/hooks/pre-push`
2. `pre-commit` will move `.git/hook/pre-push` => `.git/hooks/pre-push.legacy`
3. `pre-commit` will write `.git/hooks/pre-push`

The problem is, pre-commit only correctly invokes `pre-commit.legacy` (it doesn't yet know the correct semantics for passing along arguments).

I agree ideally there would be a way to run `git-lfs` from pre-commit itself, I'm not sure I want to support _two_ ways of running legacy hooks, but I'll put some thought into this.

I suspect if I change the current `--all-files` to have a source being the sigil "empty repository", then you could use git lfs's pre-push with the environment variables we expose... maybe...

(the main problem with trying to implement "legacy" pre-push is it's entirely different from the current execution approach.  pre-push is roughly equivalent to `./pre-push <<< "the args git sends"` instead of `./pre-push the args git sends`)

In summary there's two bits to this:
1. A bug in how pre-commit invokes `pre-push.legacy` / `commit-msg.legacy`
2. A potential feature request to add a "legacy"-like execution model.
    - A note that this already kind of exists for:
        - `pre-commit` (`pass_filenames: false` + `always_run: true`)
        - `commit-msg` (all executions of `commit-msg` are akin to the git calling convention).
    - pre-push is the only one where there isn't an equivalent

> http_data_cb does not return data chunks. It will be called arbitrarily

My own understanding of tags is this:

* For third party users only - official jupyter projects shouldn't use them
so nbconvert shouldn't use them.
* Third parties *should* use some sort of namespacing
* Third parties can interpret them in any way they want
* Frontends can only use tags in a completely generic manner (tag based
filtering for example) and should not have particular UI/UX behavior
associated with "special tags".
​
The counterside of this is:

* Anything tag like that we want to (official jupyter projects) do should
go into cell metadata and be added to the notebook format. This will
prevent us from creating informal pseudo-specifications.

Based on this, I agree with Kyle that `raises-exception` should be added to
the schema and documentation. But I would prefer it be moved to metadata.

This is an issue of your math being interpreted as HTML. Your `<k` is being interpreted as opening a `<k>` HTML tag, which then includes the rest of the document, hence the apparent truncation. There are subtle differences (not intentional ones) in how we handle math in markdown between the browser and nbconvert. In this particular case, you can avoid the issue by putting space around `<`: use `j < k` instead of `j<k` and it should be happy.

The line you refer to is correct. The images fed to the `predictions` method are before preprocessing, e.g. they have values in [0, 255].

There is two ways to do preprocessing: either it's implemented in the respective framework and therefore part of the model (in this case, Foolbox does not need to handle preprocessing at all, e.g. its preprocessing argument is `(0, 1)`) or the preprocessing it not part of the model, then it's done by Foolbox.

TensorFlow (unlike other frameworks) makes it quite easy to add the preprocessing to the model and consider the new model as one that doesn't need preprocessing. The option to specify the preprocessing Foolbox is mostly relevant for other frameworks where it's not so easy to add the processing in the respective framework.

Thanks for asking. I think have a set/get function to retrieve the keys from a know location is a good idea. In addition...

`// being handwavy partial wishlist`

*Every* ID gets:

- two keypairs generated, a signing keypair, and an encryption keypair.
- Optional: the keypairs are generated from a passphrase converted to a long deterministic seed via a KDF so you don't have to manage private key files, but instead enter a passphrase which, through a strong KDF like `scrypt` or `argon2` creates those keys in memory on the fly. Would allow use on multiple machines, impossible to lose private key. Key rotation is trickier though.
- the public keys are stored in a known location in the zone file, and marked with a tag which indicates what kind of key it is and how it is serialized. e.g.

```
{
    "pubkey_sig": "MHhERUFEQkVFRg==",
    "pubkey_sig_type": "ed25519",
    "pubkey_enc": "MHhCRUVGREVBRA=="
    "pubkey_enc_type": "x25519-xsalsa20-poly1305",
}
```

The tags allow software to determine which encryption/signing alg to use for the binary key material that is presented. Its important of course that implementations in different langs can consume those binary keys directly.

Keys are urlsafe Base64 encoded version of the binary byte array form of the key (only 33% larger than the binary form, as opposed to hex which is 2x the byte size).

The descriptive tag could also be included by prepending, with a delimiter like a colon, to a Base64 encoded version of the binary key. e.g. `ed25519:MHhERUFEQkVFRg==` if you wanted to save the whole thing in a single key.

I would not personally choose secp256k1 (aka bitcoin crypto) as the primitives for signing unless it is needed for some interoperability. If you really want ECDSA perhaps use it with a curve considered safer by the wider academic cryptography community which apparently considers secp256k1 questionable or unsafe depending on who you ask. For info on an implementation of those encryption types that I referenced for examples see:

https://github.com/dchest/tweetnacl-js
https://tweetnacl.cr.yp.to/tweetnacl-20140917.pdf
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Argon2
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scrypt

If you wanted to see an example of what I was talking about with using the KDF to generate keys deterministically, see for example:

https://github.com/grempe/session-keys-js

And while I'm at it, something to anchor your keys from a PKI trust perspective would be nice (too bad its not ready for prime time yet):

https://security.googleblog.com/2017/01/security-through-transparency.html

If *every* blockstack ID had these keys available to them for both signing and encryption, at a well known property in the zonefile, and with a key type that is self-documenting and can be changed over time without breaking older zone files, I think you open up a lot of use cases.

Ah, I see. Thanks for the swift and detailed reply, that's unfortunate load does not support LOB.

Is multi-statement mode less performant than multiload? If so, then I was thinking to go about this use case in a 2-step process:

1. Load data to a target table where the originally JSON column is a varchar, which does appear to be supported [according to Teradata](https://www.info.teradata.com/HTMLPubs/DB_TTU_16_00/index.html#page/Teradata_Data_Types/B035-1150-160K/zqb1472240532246.html). 

> In general, you cannot import or export LOB JSON data using FastLoad, MultiLoad, or FastExport protocols using either the legacy stand-alone load tools or Parallel Transporter. However, you can use these utilities to load or unload JSON data in the following cases:
> • You can load JSON data if it is less than 64 KB, and the target table defines the column as CHAR or VARCHAR.
> • If you set the transform group for the user to use a transform group that converts JSON to/from VARCHAR or VARBYTE.

2. Do an INSERT into the final table, SELECTing from the table in step 1, but using `NEW JSON()` to convert the varchar over.

This isn't particularly ideal, but I'm hoping it should allow a way forward. Unfortunately I'm not too well versed in the C++ TPT API so I'm not sure what changes could be made in giraffez that would make the general process easier.

Adding further thought on how to split those 2 exceptions, I think defining those exceptions clearly in the docs is the this is the best way to do so, rather than removing one of them completely.

- **UnsupportedOperation**: This exception will only be raised when an endpoint does not support the underlying HTTP method, that means: HEAD, POST, GET, PUT, PATCH, DELETE. This would mean that if for example we send POST but it requires some specifics, for example empty json {}, as in the case of ASM Tasks. We would NOT use this exception when coding.

- **UnsupportedMethod**: This exception will only be raised if a given method is not supported for specific endpoint, for one reason or the other. This exception MUST NOT imply that the underlying HTTP operation is completely unsupported, only THAT the class method will not work.


@caphrim007 @zancas @pjbreaux @jasonrahm 

Refactoring the code to adhere to the above will result in some of the exceptions changing, so will break backward compatibility.

This is what we are using that works. In our website we use ember app just for the basket, which uses oscar-api.
Checkout and other pages are rendered from the server along with basket's content.
Also, we don't use the Session-Id protocol and HeaderSessionMiddleware. 
HeaderSessionMiddleware works and we were using it when the app was completely a SPA using ember. Django Session Authentication works well except when the API domain is different than the website domain.

```
from django.conf import settings
from oscar.apps.basket.middleware import BasketMiddleware as CoreBasketMiddleware
from oscar.core.loading import get_model
from oscarapi.basket import operations

Basket = get_model('basket', 'basket')

class BasketMiddleware(CoreBasketMiddleware):
    """
    Here we manage the basket in session so that it will work for both api and browser
    
    Following steps will be taken
    1. Check for OPEN basket in session, if found use the same.
    2. If user is authenticated, merge session basket and remove the basket from session

    """

    def get_basket(self, request):
        """
        Return the open basket for this request
        """
        if request._basket_cache is not None:
            return request._basket_cache
        
        # get anonymous basket before actual basket
        # else operations.prepare will overwrite the session basket with user basket
        anonymous_basket = operations.get_anonymous_basket(request)

        basket = operations.get_basket(request, True)

        # since the operations.get_basket doesnt merge the
        # anonymous basket with user basket, we need to check and merge here
        if request.user and request.user.is_authenticated():
            if anonymous_basket and anonymous_basket.id != basket.id:
                self.merge_baskets(basket, anonymous_basket)

        return basket


    def process_response(self, request, response):
        return response
```

I ran into this as well and wrote a small msbuild target for this.

Basically, after the Publish target (which will publish the assets targeting the shared framework), invoke msbuild for each of the RuntimeIdentifiers specified.

```xml
<Project ToolsVersion="15.0">
  <Target Name="PublishAll" AfterTargets="Publish" 
          Condition="'$(RuntimeIdentifier)'=='' and '$(RuntimeIdentifiers)'!=''">
    <ItemGroup>
      <RIDsToPublish Include="$(RuntimeIdentifiers)" />
    </ItemGroup>

    <MSBuild Projects="$(MSBuildProjectFullPath)" Targets="Publish" 
             Properties="RuntimeIdentifier=%(RIDsToPublish.Identity)" 
             RemoveProperties="RuntimeIdentifiers"/>
  </Target>

  <Target Name="PublishRID" AfterTargets="Publish" 
          Condition="'$(RuntimeIdentifier)'!=''">
    <Message Importance="High" Text="Published $(RuntimeIdentifier)" />
  </Target>
</Project>
```

You can import this target into your .csproj and then `dotnet publish` will publish for all of the RIDs defined in your project.

In general, I support the approach of having users download a single script instead of cloning our entire repo.

With that said, I think anyway we go about this is going to increase complexity and make it harder for our users to understand what the script is doing. The two approaches I've seen proposed so far is this and the `letsencrypt-auto` super script that includes all of the OS bootstrap scripts as well.

If we did the latter, I think the plan was we would ideally write a script that pieced together the OS bootstrap scripts and `bootstrap/venv.sh`. We could have each script be a function in the super script and just piece together the functions accordingly.

Between the two approaches, I think the large bash script is easier to understand as the user doesn't have to track down all of the scripts that were run on their system. With that said, this approach proposed here is easier for us to implement and gives us more flexibility of updating the script in the future. Neither approach is completely self updating though as the initial script the user runs (correctly) doesn't try to update itself.

Despite the extra effort involved, I personally am leaning towards a cleanly written and documented `letsencrypt-auto` script with the OS bootstrap code included. With that said, I think either of these approaches is better than having users clone our entire repo.

In case it may be useful to someone else, I've used a small bash script to help me split my numerous config files:

```bash
#!/bin/bash
set -e

NAME=$1

#csplit -f $NAME.conf $NAME.conf '/^<VirtualHost \*:443>$/'
csplit -f $NAME.conf $NAME.conf '/^<IfModule mod_ssl\.c>$/'
mv $NAME.conf00 $NAME-80.conf
mv $NAME.conf01 $NAME-443.conf
a2dissite $NAME.conf
a2ensite $NAME-80.conf
a2ensite $NAME-443.conf
```

It uses the `csplit` program (in the coreutils package in Debian jessie for example) to find a pattern where the file is to be split.

Depending on your config files, the pattern can be `/^<VirtualHost \*:443>$/` or `/^<IfModule mod_ssl\.c>$/` or something else. (adapt the csplit line in the script accordingly)

The convention assumed here is that:
 - the original filename of the config is `mysite.conf`
 - you call the script above as follow `./split_vhosts.sh mysite`
 - the resulting files are called `mysite-80.conf` and `mysite-443.conf` (assuming the first VHost in the original config file was for port 80 and the second for port 443)
 - It calls `a2dissite mysite.conf` to deactivate the original conf file
 - it calls `a2ensite` for each of the split files.

Now it's still your job to check that the resulting files are correct and to reload apache. The original config files are not deleted, you may want to do it manually once you've made sure everything works.

I don't think it's a good idea to embed large objects into messages:

* If the large object is sent in-band, then it will also be received in-band. Imagine someone is fetching messages from the server and it takes 10 minutes just to get the unread messages because one message is very large. Or someone sends you a video and you have no choice but to use mobile data to download a 100MB video because it's embedded in the message. I think it's a better user experience if large media downloads are separate from receiving messages and if the user can control when and what to download. I think WeChat & WhatsApp & Telegram got it right - you get a preview in-band and then choose to download the whole thing out-of-band.

* Overall database operations over a dataset with large objects embedded will be slower compare to the same objects stored separately. For instance, you can store the messages on SSDs and large objects on spinning disks. If the objects are embedded, you have no such option. 

As for adding MongoDB as a storage option - i think it's a good idea.

The client already has built-in support for this by two means:
 * resuming from committed offsets
 * if no committed offsets are available or they are out of date: revert to the `auto.offset.reset` policy.

Which means you only need to decide if you want to start reading the oldest (`auto.offset.reset=beginning`) or latest (`auto.offset.reset=end`) if no committed offsets are available, and you do this by configuration alone.

|---|---|---|
| **RAISE** | Schema error | The field key is not declared on the schema. |
| **INCLUDE** | Field error | The field does not allow a value during load. |
| **EXCLUDE** | No error, No field value | The value was discarded during the schema validation. |

This is a very good comment, because actually newton method should be very reliable for this problem:

>for 'newton' aka 'fast' I'm concerned about the implication in the code comments that you shouldn't trust the method. Can we do something here to help the user decide if they are at risk of a bad calculation? Maybe this would be an enhancement best left for future work.

Yes, I will explain that the "newton" method converges on the value of the newton step and not the residual, so there's a possibility its answer isn't as accurate as the user needs. However, this probably would never be a concern for pvlib-python because this typically occurs in curve fitting problems when the governing equations are not a good fit for the actual raw fitting data. So this might be an issue if we were using newton to generate SDM coefficients, but in that case, first we would have much more data than equations so that would be a LSQ problem, and second we could not use newton because we would need a [Levenberg-Marquard solver](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Levenberg%E2%80%93Marquardt_algorithm).

The other concern for users is that newton can be fussy about very flat solution space (singular or stiff Jacobian) and poor initial guesses, but this would only happen if it were a _multivariate_ problem, not a single input, single output problem, which only has a 1x1 Jacobian. This problem cannot be "stiff" by definition, although it could have a derivative of zero. That would be the only concern, and "newton" raises a `RuntimeError` in that case. Regarding poor initial guess, the IV curve of a single cell is continuous and usually monotonic, and the machine precision is `np.finfo(float).eps = 2e-16` so there really is no possibility of poor initial guess again in the case of finding IV curve points. Again poor initial guess would only really be an issue for a multivariate problems, not this one. It could happen if the user chooses a point far outside of the 1st quadrant, and in that case "newton" also raises a `RuntimeError` with a message that it iterated more than 50 times and couldn't find a solution.

So the only real concern for users would be (1) if the derivative is zero and (2) if they choose an curve point more than +2*Voc outside the 1st quadrant. But I challenge any solver to find an accurate solution at more than +2*Voc into the fourth quadrant! So I guess what we need to tell people is what to do if they get a `RuntimeError`. If anyone else has any other concerns about using Newton's method let me know so I can put that in.

Also when I get back to this, I will definitely change the method names to be more descriptive of the actual method being used, _eg_: "brentq", "newton", "lambertw", etc., although it's worth noting that even the "lambertw" method also a custom [`pvlib.pvsystem._golden_sect_DataFrame` method](../blob/master/pvlib/pvsystem.py#L1737)

The reason I chose not to support lists is that it is unclear how setting a list element should interact with many list operations like append and delete. At which point in the construction of the list should the element be fixed? 

My usual workaround would be to use a dict instead (note the string keys):
``` python
@experiment.config
def config():
    model_sequence = {
        '1': dict(name='a', model_id='5943a94ba421dc1600fdb4e2'),
        '2': dict(name='b'),
        '3': dict(name=b'),
        '4': dict(name='c', a_min=1e-6, a_max=1 - 1e-6),
        '5': dict(name='evaluate')
    }
```

I have thought about adding list support, but I'm not sure what the best way would be. Currently, my favorite approach would be to allow setting list elements and to fail dynamically if the length of the list is somehow altered during the configuration. A word on copy/paste vs. keeping the commit history: We may not need all of the commit history in Lasagne/master anyway. I think the history is mostly good for the following: I find some piece of code that I don't understand/don't see a reason for, I use `blame` to see which commit is responsible for that, and then have a look at what that commit was about, i.e., all the changes that went along with the one I don't understand. For this to work, commits should be "low cohesion, high coupling" (if you can apply that concept to commits): each should include all changes needed to achieve some step of the larger goal. Ideally, a commit should neither do many independent steps at once, nor do a partial step. Both makes it harder to see what a commit was about (for the former, because there's too many unrelated changes, for the latter, because it only shows part of the picture). That's why small fixes should be amended to the correct existing commit rather than becoming a separate commit, and that's why for large refactorings, squashing everything into a single commit is suboptimal.
This being said, if the RNNs only add files and not change any existing code, I think the history is not critically important. It could be seen as a single step of adding recurrent layers to Lasagne.

So there's definitely some miscommunication, and possibly some misdocumentation that I'm going to have to defer to @ltfish about, but here are my observations:

- Your sample output is a list, but `get_all_predecessors` returns a dict, and [has not been modified for the last year](https://github.com/angr/angr/blame/master/angr/analyses/cfg/cfg_base.py#L270)
- `get_all_predecessors` is actually a very thin wrapper over [networkx.dfs_predecessors](https://github.com/networkx/networkx/blob/9f227e4e111c648c77efcd4de18fef662184adc4/networkx/algorithms/traversal/depth_first_search.py#L133), which performs a depth first search _forward_ from the given node, and returns a dictionary mapping each node found in the search to its predecessor
- If you want to just find the normal, immediate predecessors for the given node, you should use `cfg.get_predecessors(node)`, or just `node.predecessors`. If you want to find all the nodes that precede the given node, it does appear that there's no current method to do that on cfg.
- The reason we have `get_all_nodes` vs `get_any_node` in a CFG is to handle context sensitivity, a concept that only makes sense in the context of a cfgaccurate.

I highly suspect that get_all_predecessors is a misnamed/misdocumented function. @ltfish? I highly doubt that anyone is using these functions considering how strange they appear to be, and I would support changing them to actually return full lists of successors/predecessors.

Perhaps using the Pathlib module which was introduced with Python 3 can help here.
https://docs.python.org/3/library/pathlib.html

For example if i use it with a 'posix' Path on a Windows machine, it automatically converts to a `pathlib.WindowsPath` object.
```
from pathlib import Path

p = Path('some/posix/path')
print(str(p))
```

Results in:

`some\posix\path`

I suspect it also works the other way around when on Linux.

In my limited experience with the Pathlib module i find it very convenient. Also for joining paths, checking if they exists etc. The only downside is that you have to use str(Path) when you want to pass it on to something which expects a string instead of a Path object.  Which makes sense of course, but it requires some changes in the code. For example

`gdal.Open(path) # where path is a string`

Would become:

`gdal.Open(str(path))  # where path is a Path object`
I believe this is fixed, so I'm closing it for now. Feel free to reopen this if necessary.

(full disclosure: I work with @l2ol33rt) Probably the easiest way to work around it is to move the session to some kind of function that caches the result. That way it's possible to completely drop the session argument to those functions, but still get the speedup.

```python
def get_session():
  if get_session._cached_session is not None:
    return get_session._cached_session
  # otherwise create the session object, save that and return it

get_session._cached_session = None
```

@japrogramer I honestly haven't used Django since 1.4 (about 6 years ago), so I don't have too much to offer about specifics.

However, my hunch is that Django's `signals` concept and the `rx` concept are pretty similar in theory, but quite different in implementation. You'll probably need some glue to connect the `rx.Observable` object and the Django signal.

I did notice in your code that you're defining `class MyObserver`. If I'm imagining  correctly, that you lifted that from the tests [here](https://github.com/graphql-python/graphql-core/blob/master/graphql/execution/tests/test_subscribe.py#L78) I'd point out two things:
1) you probably don't need to define a class **
2) the test code isn't even using that class. It should be dropped from the file.

** see this note from the [rxpy docs](https://github.com/ReactiveX/RxPY):
> Most of the time you will not want to go through the verbosity of implementing your own Observer. You can instead pass 1 to 3 lambda arguments to subscribe() specifying the on_next, on_complete, and on_error actions.

```python
from rx import Observable

source = Observable.from_(["Alpha", "Beta", "Gamma", "Delta", "Epsilon"])

source.subscribe(on_next=lambda value: print("Received {0}".format(value)),
                 on_completed=lambda: print("Done!"),
                 on_error=lambda error: print("Error Occurred: {0}".format(error))
                 )
```

Given that you want cross-run data, this isn't something that any of our existing plugins handle—but the infrastructure should be sufficient to create the plugin that you want. Here's how I'd suggest structuring it: on each run, emit a (single) summary containing the run's hyperparameters.* Then, create a plugin that reads this summary datum from each run in which it exists, and aggregates it however you want. (The simplest way to do this would be to just throw all the hyperparameter values into a list.) Does this makes sense?

* I suppose that you could even stash these in the graph, which might be good for a proof-of-concept but eventually sub-optimal.

If you just want the ability to manually add a paragraph that's displayed somewhere in the UI, you could fork the text plugin to make some "deduplicating message plugin," such that when the message is constant across all runs you only see one copy of it.

> What should the plots do for 0 values or negative numbers? Should those just be omitted entirely? Should there be some kind of warning on the plot?

Simply not showing them would be a reasonable option -- better than what we currently have. It's always easy to switch back to linear scaling if necessary.

I think my favorite option would be ignoring them in setting axis-boundaries and then showing vertical lines diverging off the screen, like matplotlib:
```
>>> import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
>>> plt.semilogy([1, 2, 3, 4, -1, 6, 7, 8, -1, 9, 10])
```
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1217238/36570959-5858df5c-17e9-11e8-96fc-bdce3d15665f.png)

One thing that would be best to avoid is simply dropping invalid plots and showing straight lines between the remaining valid values. This could led to the misleading impression that there are no negative values.

> Do people think there's any value at all in the existing ModifiedLog scale (maybe with an adjusted base point), i.e. would they want to see two different scale options?

I have been doing scientific visualization for over a decade now. I've used a log scale many times, but I can only think of one case where I've seen a variation on a modified log-scale used (and it wasn't in machine learning). It's just not a standard visualization technique. So my preference would be to for dropping it entirely, though I would not be strongly opposed to keeping it as an option.

@Caoimhinmg, the problem with using .matplotlibrc is that different backends are required for different plotting programs, so it isn't helpful to globally set a backend in a directory.  This is generally solved in PmagPy in two ways:  each program that uses matplotlib calls matplotlib.use at the top of the file (takes priority in the developer install), and programs/\_\_init\_\_.py sets the backend to TKAgg unless it finds the program name in the list of WXAgg-requiring programs in programs/program_envs.py (takes priority with the pip install, since \_\_init\_\_.py is run before the specific program code).  

All that to say, I don't think there is a matplotlib backend issue generally at this point, and the student's issue was very specific to her OS + VM combination.

Hi Staano,

First the short answer:
Change your last line (the prediction step):
`predY = model.predict(test_x)`
to
`predY = model.predict(test_x[:])`

TL;DR;
When you call 
`test_x, test_y = image_preloader(TEST_DATA_FILE, image_shape=(IMG_SIZE,IMG_SIZE), 
                                        categorical_labels=True,normalize=True)`

your test_x and test_y do not contain your data yet. 
They are but Instances of the class ImagePreloader and LabelPreloader. Only when you want to receive data from those classes (e.g. by doing test_x[1]) they will load the date (e.g images from a given path) on the fly.

The whole purpose of the Preloader classes is to be able to handle large datasets, since one may be limited by memory (RAM) and it may not be possible to load the whole dataset at once. Thats when you want to use those classes and only load a part of the data on the fly. For example during the training phase only the batches will be loaded into the RAM when using a Preloader class.

However the prediction step does not seem to support this possibility yet.

Template/merge implementation underway in the [feature_templates](https://github.com/anymail/django-anymail/tree/feature_templates) branch. All four current ESPs are there.

Briefly:

```python
message.template_id = "ESP-template-name-or-id"
message.template_data = {
    "mike@example.com": {"name": "Mike", "order_number": 12345},
    "alice@example.com": {"name": "Alice", "order_number": 54321},
}
message.template_global_data = {
    "site": "Anymail Example",
}
```

[Some docs](http://anymail.readthedocs.io/en/feature_templates/sending/templates/) are also ready. (Still need to add ESP-specific notes: Postmark doesn't support batch send or per-recipient `template_data`; Mailgun doesn't support stored templates or `template_id`.)

I'm debating changing the data attribute names to `merge_data` and `merge_global_data` (or `merge_defaults`), since merging is often available independently of using ESP stored templates.

Also, I'm considering a few additions to the SendGrid implementation. SendGrid's API wants you to include merge variable delimiters in your merge data keys. That is, where for other ESPs you'd specify merge data as `{'name': "Mike", 'order_number': 12345}`, with SendGrid you'd need to use `{'%name%': "Mike", '%order_number%': 12345}` (if you're using `%` to mark merge vars in your template). 

That makes SendGrid's merge feature flexible -- you can use whatever template variable syntax you want -- and simple for them to implement (just simple string replacement). But I suspect it will be error-prone in Anymail, particularly if you're switching between ESPs. I'm thinking about adding a warning in the SendGrid backend if you try to use merge variable names without any delimiters, and/or a setting that automatically adds your choice of delimiters to the names.

I'm not sure I follow.  The python overridding class code wouldn't do anything special:
```python
class Sub(Base):

```

> ... but what about objects? Does functions returning references to objects will have to be use the same scheme?

No, all of this only applies to primitive types where a cast already involves a copy, which means numeric types, strings, tuples, std::complex, and a few others.  Pybind-registered types can (and should) continue to be returned by reference or pointer through the existing PYBIND11_OVERLOAD* macros.

> we introducing huge run-time penalty of construction+copying object on each call which will have horrible impact on performance (not to mention that some types might not be copyable)

As long as the type has a move assignment operator, it'll be invoked rather than the copy assignment.  (And again, these _REF macros are only intended for referenced types where pybind11 is already doing some sort of python value -> C++ instance copy operation).

> if overriding both functions returning the references to primitive type and to objects have to use the same approach then i think it would be better just disallow overriding functions returning references to primitive types but allow functions returning references to objects being overridden without such extra penalty. Does this make sense?

I understand what you're saying and I agree, but it doesn't apply with this commit: object-reference returns is still allowed with the current OVERLOAD macros--this PR doesn't change anything in that respect.

I think I see the confusion though--the macros could have a better name.  Perhaps instead of PYBIND11_OVERLOAD_REF*, one of:

- PYBIND11_OVERLOAD_BASIC_REF*
- PYBIND11_OVERLOAD_CONVERTED*

If you're curious, `certbot-auto` still installs the shim package `letsencrypt` for backwards compatibility. It also uses the command `letsencrypt` to call into Certbot for no other reason than we saw no reason to change it. Turns out that `argv[0]` is used to print the version string, so when calling into Certbot through the `letsencrypt` shim, you see `letsencrypt` rather than `certbot`.

This is a one line fix and will be changed in our next release. Thanks for reporting the problem.

The `--alias` approach would indeed solve several problems:

In my web server configuration, there is one certificate per virtual host. A customer can own one or more virtual hosts. Let's say he has domains `a.com`, `b.com` in one virtual host, and  `c.com` in another. The lineage names will be `a.com` for the first two domains and `c.com` for the third domain.

Now, assume that the customer moves `a.com` from the first to the second virtual host. If new certs were generated from scratch, we would now get lineage `b.com`  for one virtual host, and `a.com` for the other (which also has `c.com` as an additional domain in the certificate). All of this has to be dealt with using appropriate `--expand` and `--duplicate` flags (and possibly `--shrink` in the future).

However, the easiest solution would be to assign a name per virtual host using `--alias`. In that case, I could just extract the list of domains associated to the virtual host, and tell certbot to go and replace _that_ `--alias`ed certificate with whatever is the list of domains right now, regardless of whether it is shrinking, expanding, whatever.

(An obvious choice for the alias would be something related to the virtual host document root, for example the FTP user name.)

From the output you can see that amaxon linux is based on EL6, and due to version dependency issues we can't package certbot in EPEL6.

You will need to use an EL7 (or fedora) based instance or use a virtualenv based certbot-auto on that system.

I don't think the changes to `.coveragerc` are necessary. While `tox -e cover` reports the coverage percentage for each file, it's only requiring you meet a minimum percentage for the entire `certbot` module looked at as a whole.

As for writing tests so other tests have coverage, the tests is the code that `tox -e cover` runs. It runs the tests and reports coverage on them and the code it calls. You don't need to write tests to call the tests so they get coverage because the tests are run directly by `tox`.

0. Note that `--renew-hook` does not exist anymore, they are now `pre`, `post` and `deploy` hooks.
1. This is an issue with Ubuntu package at the very most, not with Certbot itself AFAICT. So report this against Ubuntu (e.g. on Launchpad) or to the package maintainer.
2. From the doc (but maybe your certbot version is too old for that to work, especially I don’t remember if that was available for `renew-hook`):
> You can also specify hooks by placing files in subdirectories of Certbot’s configuration directory. Assuming your configuration directory is /etc/letsencrypt, any executable files found in /etc/letsencrypt/renewal-hooks/pre, /etc/letsencrypt/renewal-hooks/deploy, and /etc/letsencrypt/renewal-hooks/post will be run as pre, deploy, and post hooks respectively when any certificate is renewed with the renew subcommand.
3. You can deactivate the provided systemd timer to work around the problem, but I suspect you’re going to have to do that again upon each upgrade of certbot. Way better: you can override the systemd service that is run, see below and [here](https://wiki.archlinux.org/index.php/Systemd#Drop-in_files).

First, you need to find the timer, for instance `/etc/systemd/system/timers.target.wants/certbot.timer` on my system.

This timer trigger the execution of the renewal service, that has the same name, and is likely available at something like `/usr/lib/systemd/system/certbot.service` (just check to be sure). There you can see what exactly the service does. And you can add overrides in a folder using the service name like this `/etc/systemd/system/certbot.service.d/`. In your case, that would be:
`00-renew-hook.conf`
```
[Service]
ExecStart=
ExecStart=/usr/bin/certbot renew --non-interactive --renew-hook "/opt/my_certbot_hooks/renew_hook.sh"
```
The first empty `ExecStart=` line is for removing the one in the system unit.

`certbot-auto` on CentOS 6 has deprecated support for using Python 2.6 and it will stop working entirely in the next release.

What's going on here is that normally `certbot-auto` will walk you through installing Python 3 from EPEL6, but you've included `--no-bootstrap` on the command line so it's skipping that step and failing to find Python 3 later. This error message is misleading as the real problem is it's expecting Python 3 to be available and I'm sorry about that, but again, support is going away in the next release so this case is going away. In the next release you'd see:
```
You have an ancient version of Python entombed in your operating system...
This isn't going to work; you'll need at least version 2.7
```
If you want to work around this problem for now, try setting the environment variable `LE_PYTHON` to the name or path of your Python 2.6 executable. This will continue to work as long as you keep `--no-self-upgrade` on the command line, although doing so will exclude you from any new features and security fixes to Certbot.

I think this makes sense, and has been requested in #1250. Additionally we've discussed the design of this in #1201.
As mentioned by @bmw it's easy to implement this in a `--deploy-hook` https://github.com/certbot/certbot/issues/1201#issuecomment-253885241
That said, I agree with @pde that it's probably best for this to be a default rather than associated with an installer or a flag: https://github.com/certbot/certbot/issues/1201#issuecomment-155652280
@jmtd started implementing this: https://github.com/jmtd/letsencrypt/commit/a7a8941789e2cf9767ff1fd7e74c157f3d3e3e5a but stopped since they found it easier just to fix it in their case.
My fear with this being a flag is that you might be able to request renewal without that flag and end up in a situation where your renewed cert is now missing the cert file that you've referenced in your jabber config.
That said, I'd be pretty happy with this being something you could set in cli.ini so that all of your renewals made this file, but it wouldn't be a surprise to you that you have multiple files that contain your privkey, because you had to write your cli.ini file. This also means that if jabber ever wanted to rely on certbot they could ship this cli.ini file and it'd work out of the box.
I think we'd be happy to take a PR, but @bmw would have the most useful information on what we'd want this feature to look like.

It's important to know that --dry-run is a way to test that renewal works, and is not an actual renewal. For example it doesn't actually save these certificates, and gets them from our staging server, which is not trusted by browsers.
You may want to remove the --dry-run flag from your command, so that you do an actual renewal (now that it seems like it's successful)
You can learn more about renewal here: https://certbot.eff.org/docs/using.html#renewing-certificates

Hello @bmw, thanks for the time you passed to review my code.

I am really willing to help to improve Certbot, and extend its compatiblity. To precise my background about Certbot here, I maintain now for more than a year a Docker container allowing users to handle SSL certificates through DNS challenges. For that, I use Certbot + Lexicon. The container has it success, with 250K+ pulls, and so it leads to various opportunity of improvements on the stack. I am also an active contributor on Lexicon, with several implemented providers and optimizations about the integration testing stack. So it was just a matter of time before I start to work also on Certbot directly ^^

So I would be glad to participate to the weekly hangout, I will send a mail today about that.

About the implementation strategy for Windows, I could not be more agree with you. It is excessively complex to port on Windows a software that has been designed essentially on Linux. For instance, I have another branch behind this one, to prepare a continuous integration stack for Windows. Besides the necessity to deactivate most of the tests on this plateform, I discovered how the lock mechanism (for files AND for threads) differs between Linux and Windows. More than the half of the broken tests could be corrected by a complete redraw of the locking mechanism on Certbot to make it plateform-independent, certainly using smart context managers. Needless to say that we are talking about hundreds of impacted lines of code.

So for know, here is my plan to split my work in several PR:
1) For this PR, I will not add anything else besides the corrections required. All processes on Linux stay untouched.
2) Next PR will introduce the integration stack on AppVeyor, and deactivation of all tests that fail or block the integration test process. It will be the occasion to talk about AppVeyor or alternatives, and about the tox.ini configuration.
3) Following will be a platform-independent locking mechanism required to be 100% iso-functional on Linux. Here some deactivated tests for Windows will be enabled again.
4) Then we will discuss about the opportunity to build an official IIS plugin.

I acknowlege your recommendations considering the size of the team, and the background you have for Windows. I have some of it because of my professional activities. So it is my responsibility to ensure that any modification done for Windows is recognized as so, and is correctly documented. I already tried to separate all the compat stuff to a specific module, as you saw in my code, and I will continue in this direction. For now, the use case is Certbot executed 99,99% of the time on a Unix system, and the software design needs to respect that.

@czepluch two notes about documentation in restructured text:

1. Your in document links with `:ref:` were fine but you need to have a blank line between the anchor and the title. e.g.:

```
.. _joining-existing-token-network:

Joining an already existing token network
=========================================
```

2. The external document links should not have the `.rst` extension or have the directory specified. It can be found by the sphinx document builder and it needs to not have an extensions since docs also get compiled to html and pdf. e.g.:

```please see :doc:`Overview and Guide <Overview-And-Guide>```

> see vstinner/faulthandler@957915c , for adding testing of an environment that is declared by tox. py26 is a part of the standard tox environment list.

This is an interesting assessment, that doesn't line up with the way that I have looked at it in the past. The `py26` *factor* is part of the standard tox setup, but the env itself doesn't really exist yet, in the same way that if you would configure factors `dj18` and `dj19` to install Django versions 1.8 and 1.9 respectively, but don't put them in the envlist, the `dj18` and `dj19` factors won't run either.

Still, Tox allows envs to run that aren't declared in the configuration by just configuring them the best it can, the same as it does when envs are put in the envlist, matching whatever factors it finds; in this case, the `pyXX` factor you've specified.

This makes me wonder if tox factors that are configured in the tox config, but don't have matching envs, should in general be allowed to run without declaring any specific matching env. Unfortunately, there are complications with that idea that I'm not sure if there are good ways to handle when we start considering matching multiple factors.

For example, what do we do when there are matches, or when the order is ambiguous? If we have configured `dj17` and `py36`, should the variants combine to make a guess of `dj17-py36` or `py36-dj17`. AFAIK, the order does matter to Tox, because it might have a section for it (i.e. `[testenv:py36-dj17]`) and the section won't match if the factors aren't in the right order. Also, what should happen if there's a match for `py36-dj17-spam`, but `py36-dj17` isn't declared? Should `py36-dj17` run as well, or would it be excluded?

My inclination is that so much of this doesn't make sense when generalized that it's really only applicable to 1 factor in that mode.

-----------

> If tox-travis requires modifications to tox.ini, you lose users, because they are opinionated about tox.ini, but not opinionated about .travis.yml as long as it does the job.

Tox-Travis has its configuration in sections of the `tox.ini`. Are you suggesting even that isn't a good enough separation? I hope not, because we can't put stuff in the `.travis.yml` (we just detect the stuff that Travis does with it), and I don't see a better file to put the configuration in. Other tools, like coverage and flake8 also allow their configuration in the `tox.ini`.

I suspect that what you're meaning here is that external tools, like Tox-Travis, shouldn't require changes to the Tox configuration (the specific parts of `tox.ini` that Tox actually uses for its configuration). Since our configuration is segregated into separate sections that include `travis` at the beginning, I'm hoping that you will confirm that you think that is a sufficient separation from the standard Tox configuration.

If I'm right about all that, then the primary problem that you're pointing out is that this deprecation requires changing the Tox configuration. I agree.

-------------

So, having resolved that Tox-Travis should not require any changes to the standard Tox configuration in order to operate, I believe that we have a setting that desperately needs to be added: `envlist`. This setting will act just like the Tox `envlist`, including expansion, and it will declare all of the envs that Tox-Travis may consider when choosing which envs to run.

I believe that this setting should default to the Travis `envlist`, plus additional envs specified as `[testenv:XXX]` sections, plus automatic sections for the `pyXX` envs that are easily auto-configured because of Tox's default handling of those factors. I also think that this will resolve all of the contention brought up in this issue, because undeclared `pyXX` envs will still be able to be run without addition configuration.

This alone will require keeping the hack that adds the envconfigs to Tox during the Tox-Configure hook. I'm OK with that, and I'll likely open an issue on Tox to allow us a public method that we can use to do that without so much copy and paste of internal code and monkeypatching. But the first priority is to make Tox-Travis work the way that it should.

-------------

This doesn't do away with the deprecation entirely, though, because the deprecation also covers a even more special case: undeclared and non-standard envs. For example, right now this configuration will pass through an envlist including `py36` and `docs`, even though `docs` doesn't exist:

```yaml
language: python
python: 3.6
install: pip install tox-travis
script: tox
```

```ini
[tox]
envlist = py36

[travis]
python =
    3.6: py36, docs
```

It works this way, because the original version of Tox-Travis, before we introduced factor-matching, simply passed the string along in the `TOXENV` environment variable, and Tox did what it does with any envlist, and creates any missing envs.

What I'd like to be able to do is raise an error if somebody tries to run `docs` in this case, since it's both non-standard and undeclared.

Would that smaller breaking change be acceptable? It's the real goal of the deprecation, even though it ended up pulling in more than that. I want to be able to eliminate this odd passthru behavior that is a wart of the original release of Tox-Travis.

The `find(`) method supports all properties as filter arguments. For those properties where the HMC supports server-side filtering via query parameters in the URI, this is exploited by the zhmcclient. The remaining properties are filtered on the client side in the zhmcclient code. If the set of filter argument contains properties for server-side filtering and properties for client-side filtering, they are split up and handled accordingly. As a special case, if the only filter argument is the `name` property, a client-side name-to-URI cache is used. Overall, that is the best the zhmcclient can do, IMO.

The properties that are filtered on the server side are listed in the `{Resource}Manager.query_props` attribute. For example, partitions support the `name` and `status` properties as query parameters. If you have a need to filter by some other property, and if you do that often, and if the selectivity of that property is high, we can talk with HMC development to get an additional property supported for server-side filtering.

`object-id` (or `element-id`) is not a property that the HMC supports for server-side filtering for any resource class. I assume the reason is that the resource URI can be built directly from the object ID. If you have a need to transform the object ID into a resource URI, please let us know; that could be provided by the zhmcclient. However, I'd also like to understand where that need comes from, because the HMC references resources always by URI, as far as I recall (e.g. `nic-uris` property in Partition).

This is how I think model top pressure should be defined: `get_grid` should be told model top pressure, and create `air_pressure` arrays that conform to this. When GFS is first called (the only component that cares about model top pressure), it remembers the model top pressure, and errors if the model top pressure is not uniform. On successive time steps, it errors if the model top pressure has changed. All of this erroring will already happen automatically if the GFS code is passed in the initial model top pressure when it makes its version of the pressure arrays that we check the state pressure arrays against (because those arrays won't match). This completely avoids the need for a Sympl constant which may or may not actually be in agreement with the state.

@sietse: "yield point" is great in isolation -- the problem is that it does conflict with the python "yield" keyword, and we use it in an almost-but-not-quite-entirely-different way, which is confusing. I'm currently leaning towards switching to "check point"...

Regarding cancellation: sounds like we need to clarify the docs! The way you check for cancellation is exactly by executing a yield (/check) point. That's the whole point: a yield point might switch to another task, and it might raise `Cancelled`. If you want to check for cancellation manually, you can insert an explicit yield point with `await trio.sleep(0)`. (And there's also `trio.hazmat.yield_if_cancelled`, but don't use that – it's really only there as a primitive to use as part of constructing a yield point in complicated cases. The general goal is to keep things simple by replacing the two concepts of "schedule point" and "cancel point" with a single concept that encompasses both of them. For more details, see [this section of the design docs](https://trio.readthedocs.io/en/latest/design.html#cancel-points-and-schedule-points).)

Sure. Bam file has read pair (among others) information encoded in "flag" (https://broadinstitute.github.io/picard/explain-flags.html): 0x40 indicates R1 and 0x80 R2. In terms of paired-end quantification logic, there are two widely used packages HTSeq (https://github.com/simon-anders/htseq/tree/master) and FeatureCount (http://subread.sourceforge.net/) you might refer to. Essentially, both package assigns a count to a gene, if one or both mates are aligned.

It is a very good point. Is there a standard of how/in which entry to encode this information in the bam file?

I am very happy to bring this in line with the standard in the field. I also think that using both reads jointly can help providing evidence for the existence of a longer fragment that could be more informative in the context calling of exon-intron or exon-exon spanning fragments.

I found out this is not related to connect/bind but to socket.send. As LINGER is not defined for socket, it keeps trying to send infinitely. The fixed code will be:

    import zmq
        
    INVALID_ADDR = 'ipc:///tmp/idontexist.socket'
        
    context = zmq.Context()
    socket = context.socket(zmq.REQ)
        
    # zmq.LINGER is timeout of zmq.send()
    socket.setsockopt(zmq.LINGER, 0)
        
    socket.connect(INVALID_ADDR)
    try:
        socket.send('hello')
        
        poller = zmq.Poller()
        poller.register(socket, zmq.POLLIN)
        conn = dict(poller.poll(1000))
        if conn:
            if conn.get(socket) == zmq.POLLIN:
                print "got result: ", socket.recv(zmq.NOBLOCK)
        else:
            print 'got no result'
    finally:
        socket.close()
        context.term()

I had to do a lot of search just to have this small piece of code working. I think pyzmq could have a more pythonic way to have send/receive timeout control.
